self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

description: Exploit the bootstrap reduction and attack the base interval [1/6,1/3] by combinatorial and minimal-counterexample methods.
full_text:
I did **not** close the whole base interval \([1/6,1/3]\) in this pass. I did get several clean new reductions/lemmas that shrink the possible counterexample space, especially in the lower two subintervals.

Below \(q=1-p\), \(S=\sum_i w_i v_i\), and in the strict reduced regime
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad D=\{A:w(A)\le p\}.
\]

---

## 1. \(k\)-colour bootstrap lemma

Let \(B(r)\) denote the non-strict assertion at parameter \(r\). If \(B(kp)\) holds for an integer \(k\) with \(kp\le1\), then \(B(p)\) holds.

**Proof.** Generate \(\eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(kp)\). Conditional on \(\eta_i=1\), give \(i\) a uniformly random colour in \([k]\). For each colour \(j\), the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{i\text{ has colour }j\}}\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\). Let
\[
S_j=\sum_{i:\,\text{colour }j}w_i,\qquad T=\sum_{j=1}^k S_j.
\]
If \(T\ge kp\), then some \(S_j\ge p\). Hence
\[
k\,\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge \Pr[T\ge kp]\ge kp,
\]
so \(\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge p\). ∎

Thus the global problem is indeed reduced to the base interval \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

## 2. New layer-counting progress

### Proposition 2.1: support \(7\), lower two ranges

If \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p
\]
for all
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac14.
\]

#### Proof

Let \(D_j=\{A\in D:|A|=j\}\), and write \(d_j=|D_j|\).

---

### Case 1: \(\frac16\le p\le\frac15\)

No losing triple exists: if \(A\in D_3\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge3\).

Also the losing-pair graph has no two disjoint edges: two disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining three vertices would have total weight
\[
<2p+3p\le1.
\]
Thus \(d_2\le6\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]
So
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5.
\]
Set
\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_1'(p)=30p q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]
So \(F_1(p)>0\), proving the claim in this range.

---

### Case 2: \(\frac15\le p\le\frac14\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists, because if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1.
\]
So \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p<1.
\]
By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<3p+p\le1.
\]
By the Erdős–Gallai matching bound, \(d_2\le11\); I use the weaker bound \(d_2\le12\). Hence
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]
Let
\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_2'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1>0
\]
on \([1/5,1/4]\), and
\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]
Thus \(F_2(p)>0\). ∎

---

## 3. Support \(8\) in the lowest base range

### Proposition 3.1

If \(n=8\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le\frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

No losing \(4\)-set exists: if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1.
\]
Thus \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples are intersecting, since two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<2p+2p<1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le \binom72=21.
\]

The losing-pair graph has no matching of size \(3\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<3p+2p\le1.
\]
By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
d_2\le13.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8+8pq^7+13p^2q^6+21p^3q^5.
\]
Let
\[
F_8(p)=1-q^8-8pq^7-13p^2q^6-21p^3q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_8'(p)=30p q^6+15p^2q^5+105p^3q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_8(1/6)=\frac{115305}{1679616}>0.
\]
So \(F_8(p)>0\), proving the claim. ∎

Consequently, any strict counterexample in the lowest base range \([1/6,1/5]\) must have support at least \(9\).

---

## 4. Support \(7\), upper base range: matching obstruction isolated

### Proposition 4.1

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p\le\frac13.
\]
If the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so
\[
w(A)>1-2p\ge p
\]
with strictness coming from \(w_i<p\).

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p\le1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting: if two losing \(4\)-sets met in only one vertex, their union would be all seven vertices, and the sum of their weights would be at least \(1\), but also at most \(2p\le2/3\), impossible. Equivalently, their complements form an intersecting \(3\)-family, so
\[
d_4\le15.
\]

By assumption and Erdős–Gallai, \(d_2\le12\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Let
\[
F_3(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0,
\]
and a direct derivative check gives
\[
F_3'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1>0
\]
on \([1/4,1/3]\). Hence \(F_3(p)>0\). ∎

So a support-\(7\) counterexample in \([1/4,1/3]\), if one exists, must have **three disjoint losing pairs**.

---

## 5. Useful degree test for arbitrary-support layer attacks

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D_k\) be any losing \(k\)-uniform subfamily. Let \(d_i=\deg_{\mathcal H}(i)\), and sort the degrees:
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}.
\]
Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) satisfies \(w(A)\le p\),
\[
\sum_i d_i w_i=\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
Let \(s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\) and \(\theta=1-sp\). Since \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the left side is at least the LP minimum
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\theta d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Therefore every losing layer must satisfy the necessary condition
\[
\boxed{
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\bigl(1-sp\bigr)d_{(s+1)}
\le p|\mathcal H|.
}
\]
This is especially useful in the base ranges:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
p\text{-range} & s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\\
\hline
[1/4,1/3] & 3\text{ or }4\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/5,1/4] & 4\text{ or }5\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/6,1/5] & 5\text{ or }6\text{ at the endpoint}
\end{array}
\]

This degree test rules out many extremal-looking layer configurations that raw matching bounds allow. For example, in \(n=7\), an intersecting \(3\)-family of size \(15\) is a star, with degree sequence \((5,5,5,5,5,5,15)\). For \(p<1/3\), the degree test forbids all \(15\) triples from being losing.

---

## 6. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

This is a useful structural condition for any future perturbation argument.

Assume \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal support. Let \(x=w_i\le y=w_j\) with
\[
x+y<p.
\]
Let \(R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell\). Merge \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). By minimality, the merged instance is not a counterexample. Comparing the split and merged probabilities gives
\[
\Pr_{\mathrm{split}}[S>p]-\Pr_{\mathrm{merged}}[S>p]
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Big).
\]
Since the split instance is bad and the merged instance is good, necessarily
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
}
\]
Thus every mergeable pair in a minimal counterexample forces more residual mass in the lower window \((p-x-y,p-y]\) than in the upper window \((p-x,p]\). This is a strong anti-smoothing condition.

---

## 7. Current remaining obstruction

After combining the prior support-\(\le6\) work with the new lemmas above:

- In \([1/6,1/5]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(9\).
- In \([1/5,1/4]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(8\).
- In \([1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(7\); if support is \(7\), its losing-pair graph must contain three disjoint losing pairs.

The main unresolved piece is still an arbitrary-support layer bound that controls the tradeoff between many losing pairs and the forced scarcity of losing triples and losing higher sets.

---

\frac16\le p\le\frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

No losing \(4\)-set exists: if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1.
\]
Thus \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples are intersecting, since two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<2p+2p<1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le \binom72=21.
\]

The losing-pair graph has no matching of size \(3\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<3p+2p\le1.
\]
By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
d_2\le13.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8+8pq^7+13p^2q^6+21p^3q^5.
\]
Let
\[
F_8(p)=1-q^8-8pq^7-13p^2q^6-21p^3q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_8'(p)=30p q^6+15p^2q^5+105p^3q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_8(1/6)=\frac{115305}{1679616}>0.
\]
So \(F_8(p)>0\), proving the claim. ∎

Consequently, any strict counterexample in the lowest base range \([1/6,1/5]\) must have support at least \(9\).

---

## 4. Support \(7\), upper base range: matching obstruction isolated

### Proposition 4.1

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p\le\frac13.
\]
If the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so
\[
w(A)>1-2p\ge p
\]
with strictness coming from \(w_i<p\).

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p\le1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting: if two losing \(4\)-sets met in only one vertex, their union would be all seven vertices, and the sum of their weights would be at least \(1\), but also at most \(2p\le2/3\), impossible. Equivalently, their complements form an intersecting \(3\)-family, so
\[
d_4\le15.
\]

By assumption and Erdős–Gallai, \(d_2\le12\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Let
\[
F_3(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0,
\]
and a direct derivative check gives
\[
F_3'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1>0
\]
on \([1/4,1/3]\). Hence \(F_3(p)>0\). ∎

So a support-\(7\) counterexample in \([1/4,1/3]\), if one exists, must have **three disjoint losing pairs**.

---

## 5. Useful degree test for arbitrary-support layer attacks

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D_k\) be any losing \(k\)-uniform subfamily. Let \(d_i=\deg_{\mathcal H}(i)\), and sort the degrees:
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}.
\]
Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) satisfies \(w(A)\le p\),
\[
\sum_i d_i w_i=\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
Let \(s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\) and \(\theta=1-sp\). Since \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the left side is at least the LP minimum
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\theta d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Therefore every losing layer must satisfy the necessary condition
\[
\boxed{
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\bigl(1-sp\bigr)d_{(s+1)}
\le p|\mathcal H|.
}
\]
This is especially useful in the base ranges:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
p\text{-range} & s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\\
\hline
[1/4,1/3] & 3\text{ or }4\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/5,1/4] & 4\text{ or }5\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/6,1/5] & 5\text{ or }6\text{ at the endpoint}
\end{array}
\]

This degree test rules out many extremal-looking layer configurations that raw matching bounds allow. For example, in \(n=7\), an intersecting \(3\)-family of size \(15\) is a star, with degree sequence \((5,5,5,5,5,5,15)\). For \(p<1/3\), the degree test forbids all \(15\) triples from being losing.

---

## 6. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

This is a useful structural condition for any future perturbation argument.

Assume \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal support. Let \(x=w_i\le y=w_j\) with
\[
x+y<p.
\]
Let \(R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell\). Merge \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). By minimality, the merged instance is not a counterexample. Comparing the split and merged probabilities gives
\[
\Pr_{\mathrm{split}}[S>p]-\Pr_{\mathrm{merged}}[S>p]
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Big).
\]
Since the split instance is bad and the merged instance is good, necessarily
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
}
\]
Thus every mergeable pair in a minimal counterexample forces more residual mass in the lower window \((p-x-y,p-y]\) than in the upper window \((p-x,p]\). This is a strong anti-smoothing condition.

---

## 7. Current remaining obstruction

After combining the prior support-\(\le6\) work with the new lemmas above:

- In \([1/6,1/5]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(9\).
- In \([1/5,1/4]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(8\).
- In \([1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(7\); if support is \(7\), its losing-pair graph must contain three disjoint losing pairs.

The main unresolved piece is still an arbitrary-support layer bound that controls the tradeoff between many losing pairs and the forced scarcity of losing triples and losing higher sets.

---

description: writeup (partial): Finite-support and structural lemmas for strict reduced Bernoulli weighted-sum counterexamples in the base interval.
full_text: ## Original Problem Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let $v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that \[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\] ## Problem Solved This write-up proves a **partial theorem and auxiliary lemmas**, not the full original problem. Let \(0p]>p. \] 2. If \(n=8\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), then \[ \Pr[S>p]>p. \] 3. If \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then \[ \Pr[S>p]>p. \] 4. If \(\mathcal H\subseteq D\) is a \(k\)-uniform family of losing sets, if \(d_i\) is the degree of \(i\) in \(\mathcal H\), and if \[ d_{(1)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)} \] is the nondecreasing rearrangement of the degrees, then, with \(s=\lfloor 1/p\rfloor\), \[ p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}\le p|\mathcal H|. \] 5. Fix \(p\). Among positive weight vectors with all weights \(p]\ge p\), meaning \(\Pr[S>p]p]=1-\Pr[S\le p]>p. \] ### 2. Combinatorial estimates We use three elementary combinatorial estimates. #### 2.1. Intersecting graphs If a graph on \(N\ge4\) vertices has no two disjoint edges, then it has at most \(N-1\) edges. Indeed, if all edges share a common vertex, there are at most \(N-1\) edges. Otherwise, choose two edges \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{a,c\}\), and suppose there is an edge not containing \(a\). It must meet both \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{a,c\}\), hence must be \(\{b,c\}\). Then no edge \(\{a,d\}\) with \(d\notin\{b,c\}\) is possible, since it would be disjoint from \(\{b,c\}\). Thus the graph is contained in a triangle and has at most \(3\le N-1\) edges. #### 2.2. Graphs with matching number at most \(2\) We need the following special bounds: - A graph on \(7\) vertices with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(11\) edges. - A graph on \(8\) vertices with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(13\) edges. In the polynomial estimates below we only need the weaker bound \(12\) in the \(7\)-vertex case. First, observe that any graph \(G\) on \(N\ge6\) vertices with matching number at most \(2\) has a vertex of degree at most \(2\). Suppose not, so every vertex has degree at least \(3\). Since \(N\ge6\), \(G\) contains two disjoint edges, say \(ab\) and \(cd\). Let \[ U=\{a,b,c,d\},\qquad W=V(G)\setminus U. \] Because the matching number is at most \(2\), \(W\) is independent. Pick two distinct vertices \(w,w'\in W\). Each has at least \(3\) neighbors in \(U\). Partition \(U\) into the two matched pairs \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{c,d\}\). For at least one of these pairs, the two vertices \(w,w'\) can be matched to distinct endpoints of the pair; the other matched pair then supplies a third disjoint edge. This gives a matching of size \(3\), contradiction. Hence some vertex has degree at most \(2\). Next, any graph on \(6\) vertices with at least \(10\) edges and no isolated vertex has a matching of size \(3\). Indeed, otherwise it has a vertex \(v\) of degree \(1\) or \(2\). If \(\deg(v)=1\), let \(u\) be its neighbor. Removing \(u\) and \(v\) leaves \(4\) vertices spanning at least \(10-5=5\) edges, hence containing two disjoint edges. Together with \(uv\), this gives a matching of size \(3\). If \(\deg(v)=2\), let \(u\) be one of the two neighbors of \(v\). Removing \(v\) leaves at least \(8\) edges on \(5\) vertices. Removing \(u\) from those \(5\) vertices deletes at most \(4\) more edges, leaving at least \(4\) edges on \(4\) vertices. Any \(4\)-vertex graph with at least \(4\) edges has two disjoint edges. Together with \(uv\), again we get a matching of size \(3\). Now consider a graph \(G\) on \(7\) vertices with at least \(12\) edges. If it had matching number at most \(2\), it would have a vertex \(v\) of degree at most \(2\). Then \(G-v\) has \(6\) vertices and at least \(10\) edges. If \(G-v\) has no isolated vertex, the preceding paragraph gives a matching of size \(3\). If \(G-v\) has an isolated vertex, then its remaining \(5\) vertices must span all \(10\) possible edges, so \(G-v\) is \(K_5\) plus an isolated vertex. Since \(G\) has at least \(12\) edges and \(\deg(v)\le2\), we must have \(\deg(v)=2\), and \(v\) has a neighbor inside the \(K_5\). That edge, together with two disjoint edges in the remaining \(K_4\), gives a matching of size \(3\). Contradiction. Thus a \(7\)-vertex graph with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(11\) edges. Finally, if a graph on \(8\) vertices had at least \(14\) edges and matching number at most \(2\), deleting a vertex of degree at most \(2\) would leave a \(7\)-vertex graph with at least \(12\) edges, which must contain a matching of size \(3\), contradiction. Hence a \(8\)-vertex graph with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(13\) edges. #### 2.3. Intersecting \(3\)-uniform families If \(\mathcal F\subseteq\binom{[N]}3\) is pairwise intersecting and \(N\ge6\), then \[ |\mathcal F|\le \binom{N-1}{2}. \] Here is a short proof. Place the \(N\) vertices around a circle. In any fixed cyclic ordering, there are \(N\) cyclic intervals of length \(3\). A pairwise intersecting subfamily of such cyclic intervals has size at most \(3\). On the other hand, a fixed \(3\)-set appears as a cyclic interval in exactly \(3!(N-3)!\) cyclic orderings. Since there are \((N-1)!\) cyclic orderings, double-counting pairs \((F,\text{cyclic ordering})\) with \(F\in\mathcal F\) appearing as a cyclic interval gives \[ |\mathcal F|\cdot 3!(N-3)!\le 3(N-1)!. \] Therefore \[ |\mathcal F|\le \frac{3(N-1)!}{6(N-3)!} =\frac{(N-1)(N-2)}2 =\binom{N-1}{2}. \] ### 3. The case \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\) We split into two subintervals. #### 3.1. The range \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\) No losing triple exists. Indeed, if \(A\in D_3\), then \[ 1=w(A)+w(A^c)p]\ge p+F_1(p). \] We now check \(F_1(p)>0\) on \([1/6,1/5]\). Differentiating, \[ F_1'(p)=30pq^4-1. \] On this interval, \(p\ge1/6\) and \(q\ge4/5\), so \[ 30pq^4-1 \ge 30\cdot\frac16\left(\frac45\right)^4-1 =\frac{256}{125}-1 >0. \] Also, \[ F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0. \] Hence \(F_1(p)>0\) throughout \([1/6,1/5]\), and therefore \[ \Pr[S>p]>p. \] #### 3.2. The range \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\) No losing \(4\)-set exists. If \(A\in D_4\), then \[ 1=w(A)+w(A^c)1. \] Therefore \(F_2'(p)>0\) on \([1/5,1/4]\). Also, \[ F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0. \] Hence \(F_2(p)>0\) on \([1/5,1/4]\), and therefore \[ \Pr[S>p]>p. \] Combining the two subintervals proves claim \(1\). ### 4. The case \(n=8\), \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\) No losing \(4\)-set exists. If \(A\in D_4\), then \[ 1=w(A)+w(A^c)p]\ge p+F_8(p). \] Differentiating, \[ F_8'(p)=30pq^6+15p^2q^5+105p^3q^4-1. \] For \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), we have \(p\ge1/6\) and \(q\ge4/5\), so \[ 30pq^6-1 \ge 30\cdot\frac16\left(\frac45\right)^6-1 = \frac{4096}{3125}-1 >0. \] Thus \(F_8'(p)>0\). Also, \[ F_8(1/6)=\frac{115305}{1679616}>0. \] Therefore \(F_8(p)>0\) on \([1/6,1/5]\), and hence \[ \Pr[S>p]>p. \] This proves claim \(2\). ### 5. The case \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), with losing-pair matching number at most \(2\) Assume \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\). Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning. Indeed, if \(|A|\ge5\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so \[ w(A)=1-w(A^c)>1-2p\ge p. \] Thus \[ D_j=\varnothing\qquad(j\ge5). \] The losing triples are pairwise intersecting. If \(A,B\in D_3\) were disjoint, then the remaining vertex has weight \(1. \] But \(w(A)+w(B)\le2p\le2/3\), contradiction. Hence any two losing \(4\)-sets meet in at least \(2\) points. Taking complements, the complements of the losing \(4\)-sets form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family on \(7\) vertices. Therefore \[ d_4\le \binom62=15. \] By hypothesis, the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), so, using the convenient weaker bound, \[ d_2\le12. \] Hence \[ \Pr[S\le p] \le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3. \] Define \[ F_3(p) = 1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p. \] Then \[ \Pr[S>p]\ge p+F_3(p). \] Differentiating, \[ F_3'(p)=18pq^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1. \] We verify that this derivative is positive on \([1/4,1/3]\). Put \[ r=\frac pq. \] Then \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\), and \[ 18pq^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2 = q^6(18r+15r^2+45r^4). \] Since \(q=(1+r)^{-1}\), it is enough to show \[ 18r+15r^2+45r^4>(1+r)^6. \] Equivalently, \[ H(r):=45r^4+15r^2+18r-(1+r)^6>0. \] Expanding, \[ H(r)=-1+12r-20r^3+30r^4-6r^5-r^6. \] For \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\), \[ H(r) \ge -1+12\cdot\frac13 -20\left(\frac12\right)^3 -6\left(\frac12\right)^5 -\left(\frac12\right)^6 = \frac{19}{64}>0. \] Thus \(F_3'(p)>0\) on \([1/4,1/3]\). Finally, \[ F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0. \] Therefore \(F_3(p)>0\) throughout \([1/4,1/3]\), and hence \[ \Pr[S>p]>p. \] This proves claim \(3\). ### 6. The degree-sequence test for losing uniform families Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D\) be a \(k\)-uniform family of losing sets. For each \(i\in[n]\), define \[ d_i=|\{A\in\mathcal H:i\in A\}|. \] Let \[ d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)} \] be the nondecreasing rearrangement of \(d_1,\dots,d_n\). Let \[ s=\left\lfloor\frac1p\right\rfloor, \qquad \theta=1-sp. \] Because \(0p]p]\ge p. \] On the other hand, \[ \Pr[S>p]p]-\Pr[T>p]<0. \] We compute this difference exactly. Conditioning on the two Bernoulli variables attached to \(x\) and \(y\), \[ \begin{aligned} \Pr[S>p] ={}&q^2\Pr[R>p] +pq\Pr[R>p-x]\\ &+pq\Pr[R>p-y] +p^2\Pr[R>p-x-y]. \end{aligned} \] For the merged variable, \[ \Pr[T>p] = q\Pr[R>p] + p\Pr[R>p-x-y]. \] Subtracting, \[ \begin{aligned} \Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p] ={}&pq\bigl( -\Pr[R>p] +\Pr[R>p-x]\\ &\qquad+\Pr[R>p-y] -\Pr[R>p-x-y] \bigr). \end{aligned} \] Equivalently, \[ \Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p] = pq\Bigl( \Pr[p-x0\) and the left-hand side is \(<0\), we obtain \[ \Pr[p-xp]>p; (2) if n=8 and p in [1/6,1/5], then Pr[S>p]>p; (3) if n=7, p in [1/4,1/3], and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most 2, then Pr[S>p]>p; (4) for any losing k-uniform family H with degree sequence sorted increasingly d_(1)<=...<=d_(n), and s=floor(1/p), one has p sum_{j=1}^s d_(j)+(1-sp)d_(s+1)<=p|H|; and (5) the minimal-positive-support strict-counterexample merge lemma: if x<=y are two weights with x+y
solution:
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

This write-up proves a **partial theorem and auxiliary lemmas**, not the full original problem.

Let \(0<p<1\), let \(q=1-p\), let \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\), and let \(w_1,\dots,w_n\) be positive real numbers satisfying
\[
0<w_i<p\quad\text{for every }i,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^n w_i=1.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and set
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i v_i.
\]
For \(A\subseteq[n]\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i,
\]
and call \(A\) losing if \(w(A)\le p\). Let
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[n]:w(A)\le p\}.
\]
The losing-pair graph is the graph on vertex set \([n]\) in which \(\{i,j\}\) is an edge exactly when \(w_i+w_j\le p\).

The proof establishes the following five claims.

1. If \(n=7\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\), then
   \[
   \Pr[S>p]>p.
   \]

2. If \(n=8\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), then
   \[
   \Pr[S>p]>p.
   \]

3. If \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
   \[
   \Pr[S>p]>p.
   \]

4. If \(\mathcal H\subseteq D\) is a \(k\)-uniform family of losing sets, if \(d_i\) is the degree of \(i\) in \(\mathcal H\), and if
   \[
   d_{(1)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}
   \]
   is the nondecreasing rearrangement of the degrees, then, with \(s=\lfloor 1/p\rfloor\),
   \[
   p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}\le p|\mathcal H|.
   \]

5. Fix \(p\). Among positive weight vectors with all weights \(<p\) and total weight \(1\), suppose \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal positive support to the inequality \(\Pr[S>p]\ge p\), meaning \(\Pr[S>p]<p\), and no strict counterexample with fewer positive weights exists. If two weights \(x\le y\) satisfy \(x+y<p\), and if \(R\) is the residual random sum over all other coordinates, then
   \[
   \Pr[p-x<R\le p]
   <
   \Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
   \]

This differs from the original problem because it does not characterize all \(p\) and does not treat arbitrary nonnegative weights. It proves only the listed reduced-support and structural cases, under the strict hypotheses \(0<w_i<p\), together with two auxiliary lemmas.

## Proof

### 1. Basic notation and the layer measure

Let
\[
V=\{i\in[n]:v_i=1\}.
\]
Then \(V\) is a random subset of \([n]\), with
\[
\Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{n-|A|}.
\]
Also,
\[
S=w(V).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]=\sum_{A\in D}p^{|A|}q^{n-|A|}.
\]

For \(0\le j\le n\), write
\[
D_j=\{A\in D:|A|=j\},\qquad d_j=|D_j|.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S\le p]=\sum_{j=0}^n d_j p^j q^{n-j}.
\]
Since \(w_i<p\), every singleton is losing, and of course the empty set is losing. Thus
\[
d_0=1,\qquad d_1=n.
\]

We shall repeatedly upper-bound \(\Pr[S\le p]\). Once we prove
\[
\Pr[S\le p]<1-p=q,
\]
it follows that
\[
\Pr[S>p]=1-\Pr[S\le p]>p.
\]

### 2. Combinatorial estimates

We use three elementary combinatorial estimates.

#### 2.1. Intersecting graphs

If a graph on \(N\ge4\) vertices has no two disjoint edges, then it has at most \(N-1\) edges.

Indeed, if all edges share a common vertex, there are at most \(N-1\) edges. Otherwise, choose two edges \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{a,c\}\), and suppose there is an edge not containing \(a\). It must meet both \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{a,c\}\), hence must be \(\{b,c\}\). Then no edge \(\{a,d\}\) with \(d\notin\{b,c\}\) is possible, since it would be disjoint from \(\{b,c\}\). Thus the graph is contained in a triangle and has at most \(3\le N-1\) edges.

#### 2.2. Graphs with matching number at most \(2\)

We need the following special bounds:

- A graph on \(7\) vertices with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(11\) edges.
- A graph on \(8\) vertices with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(13\) edges.

In the polynomial estimates below we only need the weaker bound \(12\) in the \(7\)-vertex case.

First, observe that any graph \(G\) on \(N\ge6\) vertices with matching number at most \(2\) has a vertex of degree at most \(2\). Suppose not, so every vertex has degree at least \(3\). Since \(N\ge6\), \(G\) contains two disjoint edges, say \(ab\) and \(cd\). Let
\[
U=\{a,b,c,d\},\qquad W=V(G)\setminus U.
\]
Because the matching number is at most \(2\), \(W\) is independent. Pick two distinct vertices \(w,w'\in W\). Each has at least \(3\) neighbors in \(U\). Partition \(U\) into the two matched pairs \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{c,d\}\). For at least one of these pairs, the two vertices \(w,w'\) can be matched to distinct endpoints of the pair; the other matched pair then supplies a third disjoint edge. This gives a matching of size \(3\), contradiction. Hence some vertex has degree at most \(2\).

Next, any graph on \(6\) vertices with at least \(10\) edges and no isolated vertex has a matching of size \(3\). Indeed, otherwise it has a vertex \(v\) of degree \(1\) or \(2\).

If \(\deg(v)=1\), let \(u\) be its neighbor. Removing \(u\) and \(v\) leaves \(4\) vertices spanning at least \(10-5=5\) edges, hence containing two disjoint edges. Together with \(uv\), this gives a matching of size \(3\).

If \(\deg(v)=2\), let \(u\) be one of the two neighbors of \(v\). Removing \(v\) leaves at least \(8\) edges on \(5\) vertices. Removing \(u\) from those \(5\) vertices deletes at most \(4\) more edges, leaving at least \(4\) edges on \(4\) vertices. Any \(4\)-vertex graph with at least \(4\) edges has two disjoint edges. Together with \(uv\), again we get a matching of size \(3\).

Now consider a graph \(G\) on \(7\) vertices with at least \(12\) edges. If it had matching number at most \(2\), it would have a vertex \(v\) of degree at most \(2\). Then \(G-v\) has \(6\) vertices and at least \(10\) edges. If \(G-v\) has no isolated vertex, the preceding paragraph gives a matching of size \(3\). If \(G-v\) has an isolated vertex, then its remaining \(5\) vertices must span all \(10\) possible edges, so \(G-v\) is \(K_5\) plus an isolated vertex. Since \(G\) has at least \(12\) edges and \(\deg(v)\le2\), we must have \(\deg(v)=2\), and \(v\) has a neighbor inside the \(K_5\). That edge, together with two disjoint edges in the remaining \(K_4\), gives a matching of size \(3\). Contradiction. Thus a \(7\)-vertex graph with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(11\) edges.

Finally, if a graph on \(8\) vertices had at least \(14\) edges and matching number at most \(2\), deleting a vertex of degree at most \(2\) would leave a \(7\)-vertex graph with at least \(12\) edges, which must contain a matching of size \(3\), contradiction. Hence a \(8\)-vertex graph with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(13\) edges.

#### 2.3. Intersecting \(3\)-uniform families

If \(\mathcal F\subseteq\binom{[N]}3\) is pairwise intersecting and \(N\ge6\), then
\[
|\mathcal F|\le \binom{N-1}{2}.
\]

Here is a short proof. Place the \(N\) vertices around a circle. In any fixed cyclic ordering, there are \(N\) cyclic intervals of length \(3\). A pairwise intersecting subfamily of such cyclic intervals has size at most \(3\). On the other hand, a fixed \(3\)-set appears as a cyclic interval in exactly \(3!(N-3)!\) cyclic orderings. Since there are \((N-1)!\) cyclic orderings, double-counting pairs \((F,\text{cyclic ordering})\) with \(F\in\mathcal F\) appearing as a cyclic interval gives
\[
|\mathcal F|\cdot 3!(N-3)!\le 3(N-1)!.
\]
Therefore
\[
|\mathcal F|\le \frac{3(N-1)!}{6(N-3)!}
=\frac{(N-1)(N-2)}2
=\binom{N-1}{2}.
\]

### 3. The case \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\)

We split into two subintervals.

#### 3.1. The range \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\)

No losing triple exists. Indeed, if \(A\in D_3\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Since weights are positive, the existence of any losing set of size at least \(3\) would imply the existence of a losing triple. Hence
\[
D_j=\varnothing\qquad(j\ge3).
\]

The losing-pair graph has no two disjoint edges. If \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{c,d\}\) were disjoint losing pairs, then the three remaining weights are each \(<p\), so
\[
1=w_a+w_b+w_c+w_d+\sum_{\text{remaining }i}w_i
<2p+3p\le1,
\]
again a contradiction. Thus the losing-pair graph is intersecting, and by the graph bound above,
\[
d_2\le6.
\]

Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge p+F_1(p).
\]
We now check \(F_1(p)>0\) on \([1/6,1/5]\). Differentiating,
\[
F_1'(p)=30pq^4-1.
\]
On this interval, \(p\ge1/6\) and \(q\ge4/5\), so
\[
30pq^4-1
\ge 30\cdot\frac16\left(\frac45\right)^4-1
=\frac{256}{125}-1
>0.
\]
Also,
\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]
Hence \(F_1(p)>0\) throughout \([1/6,1/5]\), and therefore
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### 3.2. The range \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists. If \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Hence no losing set of size at least \(4\) exists.

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting. If \(A,B\in D_3\) were disjoint, then the remaining vertex has weight \(<p\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w(\text{remaining vertex})
<2p+p\le\frac34<1,
\]
contradiction. Thus
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\). If it had three disjoint losing pairs, then the remaining vertex has weight \(<p\), giving
\[
1<3p+p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Hence, using the weaker convenient bound,
\[
d_2\le12.
\]

Thus
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]
Define
\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_2'(p)=18pq^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1.
\]
For \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\), we have \(p\ge1/5\) and \(q\ge3/4\), so
\[
18pq^5+15p^2q^4
\ge
18\cdot\frac15\left(\frac34\right)^5
+
15\cdot\frac1{25}\left(\frac34\right)^4
=
\frac{2673}{2560}
>1.
\]
Therefore \(F_2'(p)>0\) on \([1/5,1/4]\). Also,
\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]
Hence \(F_2(p)>0\) on \([1/5,1/4]\), and therefore
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Combining the two subintervals proves claim \(1\).

### 4. The case \(n=8\), \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists. If \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Hence no losing set of size at least \(4\) exists.

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting. If two losing triples were disjoint, then the two remaining vertices have total weight \(<2p\), so
\[
1<2p+2p\le\frac45<1,
\]
contradiction. Thus, by the intersecting \(3\)-uniform bound with \(N=8\),
\[
d_3\le \binom72=21.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\). If there were three disjoint losing pairs, the two remaining weights would contribute \(<2p\), so
\[
1<3p+2p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Therefore, by the \(8\)-vertex graph bound,
\[
d_2\le13.
\]

Consequently,
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^8+8pq^7+13p^2q^6+21p^3q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_8(p)=1-q^8-8pq^7-13p^2q^6-21p^3q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge p+F_8(p).
\]
Differentiating,
\[
F_8'(p)=30pq^6+15p^2q^5+105p^3q^4-1.
\]
For \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), we have \(p\ge1/6\) and \(q\ge4/5\), so
\[
30pq^6-1
\ge
30\cdot\frac16\left(\frac45\right)^6-1
=
\frac{4096}{3125}-1
>0.
\]
Thus \(F_8'(p)>0\). Also,
\[
F_8(1/6)=\frac{115305}{1679616}>0.
\]
Therefore \(F_8(p)>0\) on \([1/6,1/5]\), and hence
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

This proves claim \(2\).

### 5. The case \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), with losing-pair matching number at most \(2\)

Assume \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\).

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning. Indeed, if \(|A|\ge5\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so
\[
w(A)=1-w(A^c)>1-2p\ge p.
\]
Thus
\[
D_j=\varnothing\qquad(j\ge5).
\]

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting. If \(A,B\in D_3\) were disjoint, then the remaining vertex has weight \(<p\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w(\text{remaining vertex})
<2p+p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Therefore
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting. Indeed, if \(A,B\in D_4\) had \(|A\cap B|=1\), then \(A\cup B=[7]\), and
\[
w(A)+w(B)=w(A\cup B)+w(A\cap B)=1+w(A\cap B)>1.
\]
But \(w(A)+w(B)\le2p\le2/3\), contradiction. Hence any two losing \(4\)-sets meet in at least \(2\) points. Taking complements, the complements of the losing \(4\)-sets form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family on \(7\) vertices. Therefore
\[
d_4\le \binom62=15.
\]

By hypothesis, the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), so, using the convenient weaker bound,
\[
d_2\le12.
\]

Hence
\[
\Pr[S\le p]
\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Define
\[
F_3(p)
=
1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge p+F_3(p).
\]
Differentiating,
\[
F_3'(p)=18pq^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1.
\]

We verify that this derivative is positive on \([1/4,1/3]\). Put
\[
r=\frac pq.
\]
Then \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\), and
\[
18pq^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2
=
q^6(18r+15r^2+45r^4).
\]
Since \(q=(1+r)^{-1}\), it is enough to show
\[
18r+15r^2+45r^4>(1+r)^6.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
H(r):=45r^4+15r^2+18r-(1+r)^6>0.
\]
Expanding,
\[
H(r)=-1+12r-20r^3+30r^4-6r^5-r^6.
\]
For \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\),
\[
H(r)
\ge
-1+12\cdot\frac13
-20\left(\frac12\right)^3
-6\left(\frac12\right)^5
-\left(\frac12\right)^6
=
\frac{19}{64}>0.
\]
Thus \(F_3'(p)>0\) on \([1/4,1/3]\). Finally,
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0.
\]
Therefore \(F_3(p)>0\) throughout \([1/4,1/3]\), and hence
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

This proves claim \(3\).

### 6. The degree-sequence test for losing uniform families

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D\) be a \(k\)-uniform family of losing sets. For each \(i\in[n]\), define
\[
d_i=|\{A\in\mathcal H:i\in A\}|.
\]
Let
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}
\]
be the nondecreasing rearrangement of \(d_1,\dots,d_n\). Let
\[
s=\left\lfloor\frac1p\right\rfloor,
\qquad
\theta=1-sp.
\]
Because \(0<w_i<p\) and \(\sum_iw_i=1\), we have \(n\ge s+1\), so \(d_{(s+1)}\) is defined.

Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) is losing,
\[
w(A)\le p.
\]
Summing over all \(A\in\mathcal H\),
\[
\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
But
\[
\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n d_i w_i.
\]
Relabel the coordinates so that
\[
d_1\le d_2\le\cdots\le d_n.
\]
We claim
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
\ge
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_j+\theta d_{s+1}.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
-
\left(p\sum_{j=1}^s d_j+\theta d_{s+1}\right)\\
&=
\sum_{i=1}^s d_i(w_i-p)
+
\sum_{i=s+1}^n d_iw_i
-
\theta d_{s+1}.
\end{aligned}
\]
For \(i\le s\), \(d_i\le d_{s+1}\) and \(w_i-p<0\), so
\[
d_i(w_i-p)\ge d_{s+1}(w_i-p).
\]
For \(i\ge s+1\), \(d_i\ge d_{s+1}\), so
\[
d_iw_i\ge d_{s+1}w_i.
\]
Therefore the difference above is at least
\[
d_{s+1}
\left(
\sum_{i=1}^s(w_i-p)+\sum_{i=s+1}^n w_i-\theta
\right).
\]
Since \(\sum_iw_i=1\), this equals
\[
d_{s+1}(1-sp-\theta)=0.
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
\ge
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Combining with
\[
\sum_i d_iw_i\le p|\mathcal H|
\]
gives
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}
\le
p|\mathcal H|.
\]
This proves claim \(4\).

### 7. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

Fix \(p\). Consider positive weight vectors with all weights \(<p\), total weight \(1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. A strict counterexample means a vector for which
\[
\Pr[S>p]<p.
\]
Assume \(w\) is such a strict counterexample with minimal positive support.

Let two weights be
\[
x\le y,\qquad x+y<p.
\]
Let
\[
R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell
\]
be the residual random sum over all other coordinates.

Now merge the two weights \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). Since \(x+y<p\), the merged vector is still admissible and has one fewer positive coordinate. Let \(T\) denote the merged random sum:
\[
T=R+(x+y)u,
\]
where \(u\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)\) is independent of \(R\).

By minimality of the original counterexample, the merged vector is not a strict counterexample. Therefore
\[
\Pr[T>p]\ge p.
\]
On the other hand,
\[
\Pr[S>p]<p.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]<0.
\]

We compute this difference exactly. Conditioning on the two Bernoulli variables attached to \(x\) and \(y\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[S>p]
={}&q^2\Pr[R>p]
+pq\Pr[R>p-x]\\
&+pq\Pr[R>p-y]
+p^2\Pr[R>p-x-y].
\end{aligned}
\]
For the merged variable,
\[
\Pr[T>p]
=
q\Pr[R>p]
+
p\Pr[R>p-x-y].
\]
Subtracting,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]
={}&pq\bigl(
-\Pr[R>p]
+\Pr[R>p-x]\\
&\qquad+\Pr[R>p-y]
-\Pr[R>p-x-y]
\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]
=
pq\Bigl(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Bigr).
\]
Since \(pq>0\) and the left-hand side is \(<0\), we obtain
\[
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
\]
This proves claim \(5\).
self_declared_problem_solved: For 0p]>p; (2) if n=8 and p in [1/6,1/5], then Pr[S>p]>p; (3) if n=7, p in [1/4,1/3], and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most 2, then Pr[S>p]>p; (4) for any losing k-uniform family H with degree sequence sorted increasingly d_(1)<=...<=d_(n), and s=floor(1/p), one has p sum_{j=1}^s d_(j)+(1-sp)d_(s+1)<=p|H|; and (5) the minimal-positive-support strict-counterexample merge lemma: if x<=y are two weights with x+y
advisor_statement: Finite-support and structural lemmas for strict reduced Bernoulli weighted-sum counterexamples in the base interval.
writeup_declared_problem_solved: For 0p]>p; (2) if n=8 and p in [1/6,1/5], then Pr[S>p]>p; (3) if n=7, p in [1/4,1/3], and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most 2, then Pr[S>p]>p; (4) for any losing k-uniform family H with degree sequence sorted increasingly d_(1)<=...<=d_(n), and s=floor(1/p), one has p sum_{j=1}^s d_(j)+(1-sp)d_(s+1)<=p|H|; and (5) the minimal-positive-support strict-counterexample merge lemma: if x<=y are two weights with x+y

description: Develop a Berry-Esseen plus critical-index proof for large support.
full_text:
Below is a **large-support / regularity progress report**. I did not close every nonregular configuration, but the complementary cases are now reduced to explicit finite-head certificates plus a critical-index decay lemma, and I also get one new small-support layer-counting extension.

Throughout, reduce to the strict case
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad p\in[1/6,1/3],
\]
since any \(w_i\ge p\) immediately gives \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). Sort
\[
w_1\ge w_2\ge\cdots>0.
\]
For \(k\ge0\), write
\[
\alpha_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i,\quad
B_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^2,\quad
C_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^3,\quad
\rho_k=\frac{C_k}{B_k^{3/2}},
\]
and
\[
\sigma_k^2=pqB_k,\qquad X_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i(v_i-p).
\]

Let
\[
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
On \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\),
\[
K(p)\le K_*:=\frac{13}{3\sqrt5}<2.
\]

---

## 1. Finite-head / regular-tail Berry–Esseen certificate

For any fixed head size \(k\), condition on the head
\[
H_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i v_i.
\]
Given a head set \(A\subseteq[k]\), the tail must satisfy
\[
X_k\ge p\alpha_k-w(A).
\]
Berry–Esseen gives
\[
\sup_x\left|\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_k}{\sigma_k}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)\right|
\le C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k=:\varepsilon_k.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
G_k-\varepsilon_k,
}
\]
where
\[
G_k=
\sum_{A\subseteq[k]}
p^{|A|}q^{k-|A|}
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{p\alpha_k-w(A)}{\sigma_k}\right),
\qquad \overline\Phi=1-\Phi.
\]

This is the main regular-tail certificate: if for some \(k\),
\[
G_k\ge p+C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k,
\]
then \(B(p)\) holds.

---

## 2. A simple small-head corollary

Let \(C_{\rm BE}\le1\) for concreteness; otherwise replace constants by \(1/C_{\rm BE}\). Put
\[
\eta_0=\frac{1}{64C_{\rm BE}}.
\]
If for some \(k\),
\[
\rho_k\le \eta_0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\alpha_k\le \sigma_k,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]>p.
\]

Indeed, since \(H_k\ge0\),
\[
p\alpha_k-H_k\le p\alpha_k\le p\sigma_k\le \frac{\sigma_k}{3}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
\ge \overline\Phi(1/3)-C_{\rm BE}K_*\eta_0
> \overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac1{32}.
\]
Numerically,
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac13\approx0.0361>\frac1{32},
\]
so the right side is \(>p\) for every \(p\le1/3\).

Thus a genuinely regular tail after a head whose total mass is at most one tail standard deviation is enough.

---

## 3. One-dominant-head refinement

For \(k=1\), the finite-head certificate becomes especially clean. Let \(w=w_1\), \(\sigma=\sigma_1\), \(t=w/\sigma\). Then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
\ge
q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt)
-C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_1.
\]
Define
\[
M_p(t):=q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt).
\]
Since \(q\ge p\) and \(pt\le qt\), one has \(M_p(t)>0\) for every finite \(t\). Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_1\le \frac{M_p(w_1/\sigma_1)}{C_{\rm BE}K(p)}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
}
\]

This handles many “one large atom plus regular tail” cases even when \(w_1\) is much larger than \(\sigma_1\). The remaining difficulty is that \(M_p(t)\) decays in the far tail, so absolute Berry–Esseen is not enough when \(t\) is very large; one would need relative moderate-deviation estimates there.

---

## 4. Critical-index decay for nonregular tails

Fix \(\tau>0\). If a tail is not \(\tau\)-regular, i.e.
\[
\rho_k>\tau,
\]
then, because \(C_k\le w_{k+1}B_k\),
\[
w_{k+1}>\tau\sqrt{B_k}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
B_{k+1}=B_k-w_{k+1}^2<(1-\tau^2)B_k.
\]

Thus before the first \(\tau\)-regular tail, the tail variance decays geometrically:
\[
B_{k+r}\le (1-\tau^2)^r B_k.
\]

Moreover, if no \(\tau\)-regular tail appears after \(K\), then the whole remaining tail mass satisfies
\[
\boxed{
\beta_K:=\sum_{i>K}w_i
\le
\frac{\tau}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}\sqrt{B_K}
<\frac{2}{\tau}\sqrt{B_K}.
}
\]

Proof sketch: put \(u_j=\sqrt{B_j}\). Since \(u_{j+1}\le \sqrt{1-\tau^2}\,u_j\),
\[
w_{j+1}=\sqrt{u_j^2-u_{j+1}^2}
\le
\sqrt{\frac{1+\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}}\,(u_j-u_{j+1}),
\]
and summing telescopes.

So a long nonregular segment forces geometric decay; either a regular tail eventually appears, or the remaining tail has small total mass and the problem becomes an explicit finite-head problem.

---

## 5. Deterministic tail-filling reduction

Suppose after a head of size \(K\), the remaining raw tail
\[
T_K=\sum_{i>K}w_i v_i
\]
has total mass \(\beta_K\). We always know
\[
0\le T_K\le \beta_K,\qquad \mathbb E T_K=p\beta_K.
\]
For any residual threshold \(r\), the sharp mean/range lower bound is
\[
\Pr[T_K\ge r]\ge \psi_{\beta_K}(r),
\]
where
\[
\psi_\beta(r)=
\begin{cases}
1, & r\le0,\\[2mm]
\dfrac{p\beta-r}{\beta-r}, & 0<r\le p\beta,\\[3mm]
0, & r>p\beta.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
\sum_{A\subseteq[K]}
p^{|A|}q^{K-|A|}
\psi_{\beta_K}\bigl(p-w(A)\bigr).
}
\]

This is useful in the no-regular-tail case: critical-index decay gives an explicit small \(\beta_K\), and the last display reduces the problem to a finite weighted layer-counting check on the head.

---

## 6. New layer-counting extension: support \(9\) in the lowest base range

I can extend the previous support-\(8\) result one step.

### Proposition

If \(n=9\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

### Proof

Let \(D_j\) be the losing \(j\)-sets:
\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\}.
\]

No losing set of size at least \(5\) exists, because a losing \(5\)-set plus the four remaining weights would have total
\[
< p+4p\le1,
\]
contradicting \(\sum_iw_i=1\).

For losing pairs, four disjoint losing pairs plus the ninth vertex would have total
\[
<4p+p\le1,
\]
so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\). By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
|D_2|\le21.
\]

For losing triples, three disjoint losing triples would cover all vertices and have total at most
\[
3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus the losing triple family has matching number at most \(2\). By the Erdős matching theorem for \(3\)-graphs on \(9\) vertices,
\[
|D_3|\le56.
\]

For losing \(4\)-sets, two disjoint losing \(4\)-sets plus the last vertex would have total
\[
<2p+p<1,
\]
impossible, so \(D_4\) is intersecting. By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
|D_4|\le \binom83=56.
\]

Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le
q^9+9pq^8+21p^2q^7+56p^3q^6+56p^4q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_9(p)=
1-q^9-9pq^8-21p^2q^7-56p^3q^6-56p^4q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_9(1/6)=\frac{238705}{10077696}>0.
\]
Also
\[
F_9'(p)=
30pq^7-21p^2q^6+112p^3q^5+280p^4q^4-1.
\]
On \([1/6,1/5]\),
\[
30pq^7-21p^2q^6
=
pq^6(30-51p)
\ge
\frac15\left(\frac45\right)^6\cdot\frac{99}{5}
>1,
\]
so \(F_9'(p)>0\). Hence \(F_9(p)>0\) throughout \([1/6,1/5]\), proving the claim.

---

## Current state

The regular side is now quite explicit:

- If the whole vector is regular, Berry–Esseen proves \(B(p)\).
- If a regular tail remains after a small head, the small-head criterion proves \(B(p)\).
- If there is one dominant head atom and a regular tail, the one-head margin criterion often proves \(B(p)\).
- If no regular tail appears, the critical-index lemma forces geometric decay and reduces the problem to a finite-head layer-counting certificate with an explicit tail-filling function.

What remains is to close the finite-head certificates uniformly when the first regular tail occurs only after a large head, or when the no-regular-tail reduction leaves a large but geometrically decaying head. Absolute Berry–Esseen is too crude in the extreme-tail one-head regime; a relative moderate-deviation estimate for the regular tail looks like the next natural improvement.

solution:
Below is a **large-support / regularity progress report**. I did not close every nonregular configuration, but the complementary cases are now reduced to explicit finite-head certificates plus a critical-index decay lemma, and I also get one new small-support layer-counting extension.

Throughout, reduce to the strict case
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad p\in[1/6,1/3],
\]
since any \(w_i\ge p\) immediately gives \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). Sort
\[
w_1\ge w_2\ge\cdots>0.
\]
For \(k\ge0\), write
\[
\alpha_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i,\quad
B_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^2,\quad
C_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^3,\quad
\rho_k=\frac{C_k}{B_k^{3/2}},
\]
and
\[
\sigma_k^2=pqB_k,\qquad X_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i(v_i-p).
\]

Let
\[
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
On \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\),
\[
K(p)\le K_*:=\frac{13}{3\sqrt5}<2.
\]

---

## 1. Finite-head / regular-tail Berry–Esseen certificate

For any fixed head size \(k\), condition on the head
\[
H_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i v_i.
\]
Given a head set \(A\subseteq[k]\), the tail must satisfy
\[
X_k\ge p\alpha_k-w(A).
\]
Berry–Esseen gives
\[
\sup_x\left|\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_k}{\sigma_k}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)\right|
\le C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k=:\varepsilon_k.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
G_k-\varepsilon_k,
}
\]
where
\[
G_k=
\sum_{A\subseteq[k]}
p^{|A|}q^{k-|A|}
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{p\alpha_k-w(A)}{\sigma_k}\right),
\qquad \overline\Phi=1-\Phi.
\]

This is the main regular-tail certificate: if for some \(k\),
\[
G_k\ge p+C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k,
\]
then \(B(p)\) holds.

---

## 2. A simple small-head corollary

Let \(C_{\rm BE}\le1\) for concreteness; otherwise replace constants by \(1/C_{\rm BE}\). Put
\[
\eta_0=\frac{1}{64C_{\rm BE}}.
\]
If for some \(k\),
\[
\rho_k\le \eta_0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\alpha_k\le \sigma_k,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]>p.
\]

Indeed, since \(H_k\ge0\),
\[
p\alpha_k-H_k\le p\alpha_k\le p\sigma_k\le \frac{\sigma_k}{3}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
\ge \overline\Phi(1/3)-C_{\rm BE}K_*\eta_0
> \overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac1{32}.
\]
Numerically,
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac13\approx0.0361>\frac1{32},
\]
so the right side is \(>p\) for every \(p\le1/3\).

Thus a genuinely regular tail after a head whose total mass is at most one tail standard deviation is enough.

---

## 3. One-dominant-head refinement

For \(k=1\), the finite-head certificate becomes especially clean. Let \(w=w_1\), \(\sigma=\sigma_1\), \(t=w/\sigma\). Then
\[

\ge
q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt)
-C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_1.

\[

\[

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

description: Attack the minimal-counterexample merge obstruction.
full_text:
Let \(q=1-p\), and for a set \(A\) write \(w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i\). I use
\[
D=\{A:w(A)\le p\},\qquad D_k=D\cap {[n]\choose k}.
\]

## 1. The naive universal good-merge statement is false

The statement “for arbitrary reduced weights there is always a mergeable pair with
\[
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]\ge \Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\]
” is false.

Take \(p=0.3\), \(n=7\), and \(w_i=1/7\) for all \(i\). Every pair is mergeable, since \(2/7<p\). For any pair \(x=y=1/7\), the residual is \(R=K/7\), \(K\sim \mathrm{Bin}(5,p)\). Then
\[
(p-x,p]=(0.157\ldots,0.3]
\]
contains exactly the residual level \(K=2\), while
\[
(p-x-y,p-y]=(0.014\ldots,0.157\ldots]
\]
contains exactly the residual level \(K=1\). Hence
\[
\frac{\Pr[p-x<R\le p]}{\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]}
=\frac{\binom52p^2q^3}{\binom51pq^4}
=\frac{2p}{q}<1
\]
for every \(p<1/3\). Thus **all mergeable pairs are bad** in the merge sense. But this vector is not a counterexample: for \(p=0.3\),
\[
\Pr[S>p]=\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(7,0.3)\ge 3]\approx 0.353>0.3.
\]

So the missing ingredient cannot be a purely local merge statement. One must use the actual counterexample hypothesis \(\mu_p(D)>1-p\) quantitatively.

---

## 2. A useful conditional good-merge lemma for the two smallest weights

Let \(a\le b\) be the two smallest weights, with \(a+b<p\), and let \(R\) be the residual sum. Define
\[
L=\{T:p-a-b<w(T)\le p-b\},
\]
\[
U=\{A:p-a<w(A)\le p\}.
\]
The merge is good exactly when \(\mu_p(U)\ge \mu_p(L)\).

For \(T\in L\), define its losing upper shadow
\[
\Gamma(T)=\{k\notin T:\ w(T)+w_k\le p\}.
\]
Because \(a,b\) are the two smallest weights, every residual weight is at least \(b\). Thus if \(T\in L\) and \(k\in \Gamma(T)\), then
\[
w(T)+w_k>p-a-b+b=p-a
\]
and also \(w(T)+w_k\le p\), so
\[
T\cup\{k\}\in U.
\]

Let \(K\) be the maximum number of lower-shadow predecessors of any \(A\in U\), i.e.
\[
K=\max_{A\in U}|\{k\in A:A\setminus\{k\}\in L\}|.
\]
Then
\[
\mu_p(U)\ge \frac{p}{qK}\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T).
\]

Therefore, if
\[
\frac{\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T)}{\mu_p(L)}
\ge \frac{q}{p}K,
\]
then the pair \(a,b\) is a good merge.

Equivalently, in a minimal counterexample where every mergeable pair is bad, the two-smallest lower window must have unusually poor \(p\)-biased upper-shadow expansion:
\[
\frac{\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T)}{\mu_p(L)}
< \frac{q}{p}K.
\]
For \(p\le 1/3\), the factor \(q/p\ge 2\), so this says the lower window must be very close to maximal losing sets. This is a concrete structural obstruction that any future proof must exploit.

---

## 3. Support \(7\), upper interval closed

I can close the remaining support-\(7\) upper-interval case, including the previous obstruction with three disjoint losing pairs.

### Proposition

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p<\frac13.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Thus no strict reduced counterexample of support \(7\) exists in the upper base interval.

### Proof

Let \(W\) be the graph of **winning pairs**:
\[
ij\in W \iff w_i+w_j>p.
\]
Write \(m=|W|\). Then
\[
d_2=|D_2|=21-m.
\]

Because every \(w_i<p\), every set of size at least \(5\) is winning:
\[
|A|\ge 5
\implies w(A)=1-w(A^c)>1-2p>p.
\]
So only layers \(0,1,2,3,4\) matter.

#### Step 1: losing triples are intersecting

If \(A,B\in D_3\) were disjoint, and \(z\) were the remaining vertex, then
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z<p+p+p=3p\le 1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_3\) is intersecting, so by EKR,
\[
d_3\le 15.
\]

#### Step 2: complements of losing triples force winning-pair blockers

Let \(T\in D_3\), and let \(Q=T^c\), so \(|Q|=4\). If \(Q\) could be partitioned into two losing pairs, then
\[
1=w(T)+w(P_1)+w(P_2)\le 3p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence \(W[Q]\) must meet every perfect matching of \(K_4\). A subgraph of \(K_4\) meeting all three perfect matchings contains at least three edges; its minimal blockers are a triangle or a \(3\)-edge star.

This gives the following graph-count bounds for \(d_3\):

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
m & d_3\text{ bound}\\
\hline
m\le 2 & d_3=0\\
3\le m\le 4 & d_3\le 4\\
m=5 & d_3\le 10\\
m\ge 6 & d_3\le 15
\end{array}
\]

The \(m=5\) bound is maximized by a \(5\)-edge star, which gives \(\binom53=10\) four-sets whose winning-pair graph contains a \(3\)-star.

#### Step 3: losing four-sets are \(3\)-intersecting

If \(A,B\in D_4\) and \(|A\cap B|\le 2\), then one can choose disjoint triples \(A'\subset A\), \(B'\subset B\). Since every subset of a losing set is losing, this would give two disjoint losing triples, impossible. Therefore \(D_4\) is \(3\)-intersecting.

Equivalently, the complements of sets in \(D_4\) form a \(2\)-intersecting \(3\)-uniform family on \(7\) vertices. Hence
\[
d_4\le 5.
\]
Moreover, if \(d_3\le 4\), then \(d_4\le 1\), because one losing \(4\)-set already contributes its four \(3\)-subsets to \(D_3\), and two distinct \(4\)-sets would contribute at least seven triples.

#### Step 4: pair-sum feasibility

The sum of all pair weights is
\[
\sum_{i<j}(w_i+w_j)=6.
\]
Each losing pair has weight at most \(p\), and each winning pair has weight less than \(2p\). Hence
\[
6\le (21-m)p+2mp=(21+m)p.
\]
In particular, when \(m\le 2\),
\[
p\ge \frac6{23}.
\]

#### Step 5: polynomial check

For bounds
\[
d_2\le a,\qquad d_3\le b,\qquad d_4\le c,
\]
we have
\[
\mu_p(D)
\le q^7+7pq^6+a p^2q^5+b p^3q^4+c p^4q^3.
\]
Writing \(r=p/q\), this is
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7(1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4).
\]
Since
\[
1-p=q=q^7(1+r)^6,
\]
it is enough to prove
\[
(1+r)^6>1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4.
\]

The four cases are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
\text{case} & (a,b,c) & r\text{ range} & \text{positive factor}\\
\hline
m\le 2 & (21,0,0) & r\ge 6/17 &
-1-6r+20r^2+15r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
3\le m\le 4 & (18,4,1) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1-3r+16r^2+14r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
m=5 & (16,10,5) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1-r+10r^2+10r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
m\ge 6 & (15,15,5) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1+5r^2+10r^3+6r^4+r^5
\end{array}
\]

In each row,
\[
(1+r)^6-(1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4)=r\cdot h(r),
\]
and \(h(r)\) is increasing on the stated interval. At the left endpoints the values are respectively
\[
\frac{187075}{17^5},\qquad
\frac{91}{243},\qquad
\frac{55}{243},\qquad
\frac1{243},
\]
all positive. Hence \(\mu_p(D)<1-p\), so
\[
\Pr[S>p]=1-\mu_p(D)>p.
\]
This proves the proposition. ∎

At \(p=1/3\), the original non-strict statement \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\) is already covered by the cyclic \(p=1/k\) argument with \(k=3\). The strict \(>\) endpoint is a boundary issue and is not needed for \(B(1/3)\).

---

## 4. Consequences

Combining this with the prior support-\(7\) result for \([1/6,1/4]\), support \(7\) is now eliminated throughout the base interval \(1/6\le p<1/3\). Therefore any strict reduced counterexample with \(p<1/3\) has support at least \(8\).

The merge program remains open in arbitrary support, but the two key takeaways are:

1. **All local merge inequalities can hold in non-counterexamples**, so the counterexample condition \(\mu_p(D)>1-p\) is essential.
2. In a minimal counterexample, every mergeable pair must have a lower-window family with abnormally small biased upper-shadow expansion.

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

description: writeup (partial): Complement upper-tail and k-wise-intersection reduction for strict counterexamples to the Bernoulli weighted-sum inequality.
full_text:
## Original Problem Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let $v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that \[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\] ## Problem Solved The result proved below is the following **partial structural reduction**. Let \(m\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), let \([m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\), and let \(2^{[m]}\) denote the set of all subsets of \([m]\). Let \(w_1,\dots,w_m\in[0,\infty)\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), and for every \(S\subseteq[m]\) define \[ w(S)=\sum_{i\in S}w_i, \] with the empty sum equal to \(0\). Let \(p\in(0,1)\), and set \(q=1-p\). A Bernoulli\((t)\) random variable is a \(\{0,1\}\)-valued random variable equal to \(1\) with probability \(t\). Let \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and let \(\xi_1,\dots,\xi_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((q)\) random variables. For \(t\in[0,1]\) and \(\mathcal A\subseteq2^{[m]}\), define the \(t\)-biased measure on the discrete cube by \[ \mu_t(\mathcal A)=\sum_{S\in\mathcal A} t^{|S|}(1-t)^{m-|S|}, \] with the usual endpoint convention \(0^0=1\). Define \[ \mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[m]:w(A)q\}. \] For a positive integer \(r\), a family \(\mathcal H\subseteq2^{[m]}\) is called \(r\)-wise intersecting if, for every \(H_1,\dots,H_r\in\mathcal H\), not necessarily distinct, \[ H_1\cap\cdots\cap H_r\neq\varnothing. \] Then the following statements hold. 1. For the same fixed weight vector \(w_1,\dots,w_m\), \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i \xi_i>q\right]\le q. \] 2. The complementation map \(A\mapsto [m]\setminus A\) is a bijection from \(\mathcal D\) onto \(\mathcal F\), and \[ \mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F). \] 3. For every positive integer \(r\) satisfying \(rp\le1\), the family \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting. In particular, for every \(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. This differs from the original problem because it does not classify all probabilities \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally. It proves a reduction and structural consequence for each fixed admissible weight vector and each fixed \(p\in(0,1)\). ## Proof Let \[ V=\{i\in[m]:v_i=1\}. \] Because the \(v_i\) are independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, for every \(A\subseteq[m]\), \[ \Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{m-|A|}. \] Thus \(V\) has law \(\mu_p\). Similarly, if \[ \Xi=\{i\in[m]:\xi_i=1\}, \] then \(\Xi\) has law \(\mu_q\), since \[ \Pr[\Xi=B]=q^{|B|}p^{m-|B|} \] for every \(B\subseteq[m]\). First prove the complementation statement. For every \(A\subseteq[m]\), \[ w([m]\setminus A)=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i-w(A)=1-w(A). \] Therefore \[ A\in\mathcal D \iff w(A)

1-p=q \iff w([m]\setminus A)>q \iff [m]\setminus A\in\mathcal F. \] Thus complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\). Using this bijection, \[ \mu_p(\mathcal D) =\sum_{A\in\mathcal D}p^{|A|}q^{m-|A|} =\sum_{B\in\mathcal F}p^{m-|B|}q^{|B|} =\sum_{B\in\mathcal F}q^{|B|}(1-q)^{m-|B|} =\mu_q(\mathcal F), \] because \(1-q=p\). Now, \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right] =\Pr[w(V)\ge p] =1-\Pr[w(V)q\right] =\Pr[w(\Xi)>q] =\mu_q(\mathcal F). \] Since \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\), we get \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p \iff 1-\mu_p(\mathcal D)\ge p \iff \mu_p(\mathcal D)\le q \iff \mu_q(\mathcal F)\le q \iff \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\xi_i>q\right]\le q. \] This proves the equivalence. It remains to prove the \(r\)-wise intersection property. Let \(r\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) satisfy \(rp\le1\). Suppose, toward a contradiction, that there exist \(B_1,\dots,B_r\in\mathcal F\) such that \[ B_1\cap\cdots\cap B_r=\varnothing. \] For each \(i\in[m]\), the element \(i\) is missing from at least one of the sets \(B_1,\dots,B_r\). Hence \[ \#\{j\in\{1,\dots,r\}:i\in B_j\}\le r-1. \] Therefore \[ \sum_{j=1}^r w(B_j) = \sum_{j=1}^r\sum_{i\in B_j}w_i = \sum_{i=1}^m w_i\,\#\{j:i\in B_j\} \le (r-1)\sum_{i=1}^m w_i =r-1. \] On the other hand, since every \(B_j\in\mathcal F\), \[ w(B_j)>q=1-p \] for every \(j\), and so \[ \sum_{j=1}^r w(B_j)>r(1-p)=r-rp\ge r-1, \] because \(rp\le1\). This contradicts the previous inequality. Hence every \(r\) members of \(\mathcal F\) have nonempty intersection, so \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting. Finally, if \(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) and \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(kp\le1\). Applying the preceding result with \(r=k\), the family \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. Because all weights are nonnegative, \(\mathcal F\) is also increasing: if \(B\in\mathcal F\) and \(B\subseteq C\subseteq[m]\), then \(w(C)\ge w(B)>q\), so \(C\in\mathcal F\). Thus \(\mathcal F\) is an increasing linear-threshold family, namely membership is determined by the strict linear inequality \(w(B)>q\). This reduction is useful for strict counterexamples as follows. A strict failure of the original inequality for fixed \(p\) and weights means \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]q\). Therefore, on the reciprocal interval \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), any strict counterexample produces an increasing linear-threshold family \(\mathcal F\) whose \(q\)-biased measure exceeds \(q\) and which is \(k\)-wise intersecting. ```json { "original_problem": "Let $w_1, \\ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let \n$v_1, \\ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that\n\\[\\Pr\\Big[\\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\\geq p\\Big]\\geq p?\\]", "problem_solved": "For every positive integer m, every nonnegative weight vector (w_1,...,w_m) with sum 1, every p in (0,1), q=1-p, independent Bernoulli(p) variables v_i, and independent Bernoulli(q) variables xi_i, the inequality Pr[sum_i w_i v_i >= p] >= p is equivalent, for the same weights, to Pr[sum_i w_i xi_i > q] <= q. With [m]={1,...,m}, w(S)=sum_{i in S} w_i, D={A subset [m]: w(A)q}, and mu_t(G)=sum_{S in G} t^{|S|}(1-t)^{m-|S|}, complementation maps D bijectively to F and mu_p(D)=mu_q(F). For every positive integer r with r p <= 1, F is r-wise intersecting, meaning every r not necessarily distinct members have nonempty common intersection; hence, for every positive integer k with p in [1/(k+1),1/k], F is k-wise intersecting. This is a partial structural reduction, not a complete classification of all p in the original question.", "is_relaxation": true } ```
solution:
## Original Problem Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let $v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that \[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\] ## Problem Solved The result proved below is the following **partial structural reduction**. Let \(m\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), let \([m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\), and let \(2^{[m]}\) denote the set of all subsets of \([m]\). Let \(w_1,\dots,w_m\in[0,\infty)\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), and for every \(S\subseteq[m]\) define \[ w(S)=\sum_{i\in S}w_i, \] with the empty sum equal to \(0\). Let \(p\in(0,1)\), and set \(q=1-p\). A Bernoulli\((t)\) random variable is a \(\{0,1\}\)-valued random variable equal to \(1\) with probability \(t\). Let \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and let \(\xi_1,\dots,\xi_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((q)\) random variables. For \(t\in[0,1]\) and \(\mathcal A\subseteq2^{[m]}\), define the \(t\)-biased measure on the discrete cube by \[ \mu_t(\mathcal A)=\sum_{S\in\mathcal A} t^{|S|}(1-t)^{m-|S|}, \] with the usual endpoint convention \(0^0=1\). Define \[ \mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[m]:w(A)q\}. \] For a positive integer \(r\), a family \(\mathcal H\subseteq2^{[m]}\) is called \(r\)-wise intersecting if, for every \(H_1,\dots,H_r\in\mathcal H\), not necessarily distinct, \[ H_1\cap\cdots\cap H_r\neq\varnothing. \] Then the following statements hold. 1. For the same fixed weight vector \(w_1,\dots,w_m\), \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i \xi_i>q\right]\le q. \] 2. The complementation map \(A\mapsto [m]\setminus A\) is a bijection from \(\mathcal D\) onto \(\mathcal F\), and \[ \mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F). \] 3. For every positive integer \(r\) satisfying \(rp\le1\), the family \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting. In particular, for every \(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. This differs from the original problem because it does not classify all probabilities \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally. It proves a reduction and structural consequence for each fixed admissible weight vector and each fixed \(p\in(0,1)\). ## Proof Let \[ V=\{i\in[m]:v_i=1\}. \] Because the \(v_i\) are independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, for every \(A\subseteq[m]\), \[ \Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{m-|A|}. \] Thus \(V\) has law \(\mu_p\). Similarly, if \[ \Xi=\{i\in[m]:\xi_i=1\}, \] then \(\Xi\) has law \(\mu_q\), since \[ \Pr[\Xi=B]=q^{|B|}p^{m-|B|} \] for every \(B\subseteq[m]\). First prove the complementation statement. For every \(A\subseteq[m]\), \[ w([m]\setminus A)=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i-w(A)=1-w(A). \] Therefore \[ A\in\mathcal D \iff w(A)

1-p=q \iff w([m]\setminus A)>q \iff [m]\setminus A\in\mathcal F. \] Thus complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\). Using this bijection, \[ \mu_p(\mathcal D) =\sum_{A\in\mathcal D}p^{|A|}q^{m-|A|} =\sum_{B\in\mathcal F}p^{m-|B|}q^{|B|} =\sum_{B\in\mathcal F}q^{|B|}(1-q)^{m-|B|} =\mu_q(\mathcal F), \] because \(1-q=p\). Now, \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right] =\Pr[w(V)\ge p] =1-\Pr[w(V)q\right] =\Pr[w(\Xi)>q] =\mu_q(\mathcal F). \] Since \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\), we get \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p \iff 1-\mu_p(\mathcal D)\ge p \iff \mu_p(\mathcal D)\le q \iff \mu_q(\mathcal F)\le q \iff \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\xi_i>q\right]\le q. \] This proves the equivalence. It remains to prove the \(r\)-wise intersection property. Let \(r\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) satisfy \(rp\le1\). Suppose, toward a contradiction, that there exist \(B_1,\dots,B_r\in\mathcal F\) such that \[ B_1\cap\cdots\cap B_r=\varnothing. \] For each \(i\in[m]\), the element \(i\) is missing from at least one of the sets \(B_1,\dots,B_r\). Hence \[ \#\{j\in\{1,\dots,r\}:i\in B_j\}\le r-1. \] Therefore \[ \sum_{j=1}^r w(B_j) = \sum_{j=1}^r\sum_{i\in B_j}w_i = \sum_{i=1}^m w_i\,\#\{j:i\in B_j\} \le (r-1)\sum_{i=1}^m w_i =r-1. \] On the other hand, since every \(B_j\in\mathcal F\), \[ w(B_j)>q=1-p \] for every \(j\), and so \[ \sum_{j=1}^r w(B_j)>r(1-p)=r-rp\ge r-1, \] because \(rp\le1\). This contradicts the previous inequality. Hence every \(r\) members of \(\mathcal F\) have nonempty intersection, so \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting. Finally, if \(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) and \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(kp\le1\). Applying the preceding result with \(r=k\), the family \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. Because all weights are nonnegative, \(\mathcal F\) is also increasing: if \(B\in\mathcal F\) and \(B\subseteq C\subseteq[m]\), then \(w(C)\ge w(B)>q\), so \(C\in\mathcal F\). Thus \(\mathcal F\) is an increasing linear-threshold family, namely membership is determined by the strict linear inequality \(w(B)>q\). This reduction is useful for strict counterexamples as follows. A strict failure of the original inequality for fixed \(p\) and weights means \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]q\). Therefore, on the reciprocal interval \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), any strict counterexample produces an increasing linear-threshold family \(\mathcal F\) whose \(q\)-biased measure exceeds \(q\) and which is \(k\)-wise intersecting.

advisor_statement: Complement upper-tail and k-wise-intersection reduction for strict counterexamples to the Bernoulli weighted-sum inequality.
writeup_declared_problem_solved: For every positive integer m, every nonnegative weight vector (w_1,...,w_m) with sum 1, every p in (0,1), q=1-p, independent Bernoulli(p) variables v_i, and independent Bernoulli(q) variables xi_i, the inequality Pr[sum_i w_i v_i >= p] >= p is equivalent, for the same weights, to Pr[sum_i w_i xi_i > q] <= q. With [m]={1,...,m}, w(S)=sum_{i in S} w_i, D={A subset [m]: w(A)<p}, F={B subset [m]: w(B)>q}, and mu_t(G)=sum_{S in G} t^{|S|}(1-t)^{m-|S|}, complementation maps D bijectively to F and mu_p(D)=mu_q(F). For every positive integer r with r p <= 1, F is r-wise intersecting, meaning every r not necessarily distinct members have nonempty common intersection; hence, for every positive integer k with p in [1/(k+1),1/k], F is k-wise intersecting. description: Close the next finite-support obstruction, especially support 8.
full_text:
I was able to close **support \(n=8\)** throughout the requested base range:

\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^8w_i=1,\qquad \frac15\le p<\frac13.
\]

Equivalently, for \(D=\{A\subseteq[8]:w(A)\le p\}\),

\[
\mu_p(D)<1-p,
\]

so

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

I did **not** push \(n=9\) beyond the already known \([1/6,1/5]\) range in this pass.

---

# Theorem: support \(8\) is eliminated on \([1/5,1/3)\)

Let \(q=1-p\), \(r=p/q\), and \(d_j=|D_j|\). Since

\[
\mu_p(D)=q^8\sum_{j=0}^8 d_j r^j
\]

and

\[
1-p=q=q^8(1+r)^7,
\]

it suffices to prove

\[
\sum_j d_j r^j<(1+r)^7.
\]

---

## 1. Range \([1/5,1/4)\)

For \(p<1/4\), no losing set of size \(5\) or more exists. Also:

- four disjoint losing pairs would have total weight \(<1\), so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\), hence by Erdős–Gallai
  \[
  d_2\le 21;
  \]
- two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices have total \(<4p\le1\), so \(D_3\) is intersecting, hence
  \[
  d_3\le \binom72=21;
  \]
- the lower shadow of \(D_4\) lies inside \(D_3\). By Kruskal–Katona, if \(d_4\ge16\), then \(|\partial D_4|\ge23>21\). Hence
  \[
  d_4\le15.
  \]

Thus

\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8(1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4).
\]

But for \(r\in[1/4,1/3)\),

\[
(1+r)^7-(1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4)
=
r\bigl(-1+14r^2+20r^3+21r^4+7r^5+r^6\bigr)>0.
\]

So \(\mu_p(D)<q\).

---

## 2. Structural tools for \([1/4,1/3)\)

Let \(W\) be the graph of winning pairs,

\[
ij\in W \iff w_i+w_j>p,
\]

and put \(m=|W|\), so

\[
d_2=28-m.
\]

### High-layer complement bounds

If \(A\in D_4\), then \(A^c\) is a \(4\)-set of weight at least \(q\). If two such complements met in at most one vertex, their weights would sum to at most \(1+p\), contradicting \(2q>1+p\) for \(p<1/3\). Hence the complements of \(D_4\) form a \(2\)-intersecting \(4\)-family on \(8\) vertices. By the \(t=2\) Erdős–Ko–Rado/Frankl bound,

\[
d_4\le17.
\]

Similarly, complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting \(3\)-family, so

\[
d_5\le6.
\]

Also, every losing \(4\)-set complement must be a \(W\)-blocker: \(W\) must meet every perfect matching of that \(4\)-set. For small \(m\), direct graph checking gives

\[
m\le2\Rightarrow d_4=0,\qquad
m=3,4\Rightarrow d_4\le5,\qquad
m=5\Rightarrow d_4\le10.
\]

### Losing-triple bound

Let \(H=D_3\), \(h=d_3\). If \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint, then the complementary pair \(e=[8]\setminus(T\cup U)\) must be winning; otherwise \(w(T)+w(U)+w(e)\le3p<1\). For each winning edge \(e\), there are exactly \(10\) unordered partitions of the remaining \(6\) vertices into two triples. Hence the number \(I(H)\) of disjoint pairs in \(H\) satisfies

\[
I(H)\le10m.
\]

The Kneser graph \(KG(8,3)\) has \(56\) vertices, degree \(10\), and least eigenvalue \(-6\), so Hoffman supersaturation gives

\[
I(H)\ge \frac{h(h-21)}7.
\]

Therefore

\[
h\le H_m:=\left\lfloor \frac{21+\sqrt{441+280m}}2\right\rfloor.
\]

We also use a weight-deficiency bound. For a losing pair \(ij\), set

\[
\delta_{ij}=p-w_i-w_j\ge0.
\]

For every losing triple \(T\),

\[
\sum_{ij\subset T}\delta_{ij}=3p-2w(T)\ge p.
\]

Each losing pair lies in at most \(6\) triples, and

\[
\sum_{ij\in D_2}\delta_{ij}
\le ((28+m)p-7).
\]

Thus

\[
d_3\le 6\left(28+m-\frac7p\right)
=6\left(21+m-\frac7r\right).
\]

---

## 3. Range \([2/7,1/3)\)

Here \(r\in[2/5,1/2)\). Use

\[
d_2=28-m,\qquad d_3\le H_m,\qquad d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le6.
\]

So

\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8\bigl(1+8r+(28-m)r^2+H_m r^3+17r^4+6r^5\bigr).
\]

A finite polynomial check over \(m=0,\dots,28\) gives

\[
(1+r)^7-\bigl(1+8r+(28-m)r^2+H_m r^3+17r^4+6r^5\bigr)>0
\]

for all \(r\in[2/5,1/2)\). The worst case is \(m=0\) at \(r=2/5\), where the gap is

\[
\frac{1618}{78125}>0.
\]

Thus \(\mu_p(D)<q\) on \([2/7,1/3)\).

---

## 4. Middle range \([1/4,2/7]\)

Here \(r\in[1/3,2/5]\). Define the **very-high pair graph**

\[
E=\{ij:w_i+w_j\ge1-2p\}.
\]

If \(T,U\in D_3\) are disjoint, then their complementary pair belongs to \(E\). Also, if \(A\in D_5\), then every pair inside \(A^c\) belongs to \(E\).

For \(p\le2/7\), one checks:

\[
|E|\le6,
\]

and if \(|E|=6\), then \(E\cong K_4\). Hence:

- if \(|E|\le5\), then \(d_3\le31\) and \(d_5\le2\);
- if \(|E|=6\), then \(d_3\le28\) and \(d_5\le4\).

Combining this with the small-\(m\) blocker bounds for \(d_4\), the following table gives sufficient layer bounds. In each row, substituting the displayed bounds into

\[
(1+r)^7-\bigl(1+8r+d_2r^2+d_3r^3+d_4r^4+d_5r^5\bigr)
\]

gives a positive polynomial on \(r\in[1/3,2/5]\).

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
\text{case} & d_3 & d_4,d_5 & \text{minimum gap}\\
\hline
m=0,1,2 &
\min\{6(21+m-7/r),H_m\} &
d_4=d_5=0 &
>0
\\
m=3,4 &
\min\{6(21+m-7/r),H_m\} &
d_4\le5,\ d_5\le2 &
>0
\\
m=5 &
\min\{6(26-7/r),31\} &
d_4r^4+d_5r^5\le10r^4 &
>0.009
\\
m\ge6,\ |E|\le5 &
31 &
d_4\le17,\ d_5\le2 &
\frac{31}{2187}
\\
|E|=6 &
28 &
d_4\le17,\ d_5\le4 &
\frac{256}{2187}
\end{array}
\]

Therefore \(\mu_p(D)<q\) also on \([1/4,2/7]\).

Combining the three ranges proves:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No strict reduced counterexample of support }8\text{ exists for }1/5\le p<1/3.
}
\]

Together with the earlier \(n=8\) result on \([1/6,1/5]\), support \(8\) is now eliminated on the entire base interval \([1/6,1/3)\).

---
