description: writeup (partial): Strict reduced support-9 theorem on the base interval: for every p in [1/6,1/3), every nine positive weights 0p]>p for iid Bernoulli(p) variables.
full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

This proof establishes the following support-$9$ strict-reduced theorem.

Let \(p\) be a real number with
\[
\frac16\le p<\frac13.
\]
Let \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) be positive real numbers such that
\[
w_i<p\quad\text{for every }i\in\{1,\dots,9\},
\qquad
\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i=1.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_9\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, i.e.
\[
\Pr[v_i=1]=p,\qquad \Pr[v_i=0]=1-p.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

This is not the full original problem: it proves a strict support-\(9\) special case, with the additional hypotheses \(m=9\), \(0<w_i<p\), and with the strict event \(>p\).

## Proof

Let
\[
q=1-p,\qquad r=\frac pq.
\]
For \(A\subseteq[9]:=\{1,\dots,9\}\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]
Define the losing family
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[9]: w(A)\le p\},
\]
and its layer counts
\[
d_j=\left|D\cap\binom{[9]}j\right|.
\]

Let \(V=\{i:v_i=1\}\). Then
\[
\Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{9-|A|}
=q^9 r^{|A|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=\sum_{A\in D}p^{|A|}q^{9-|A|}
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j.
\]
Thus
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]>p
\]
is equivalent to
\[
q^9\sum_{j=0}^9d_jr^j<q.
\]
Since \(1+r=1/q\), we have
\[
q^9(1+r)^8=q.
\]
Hence it suffices throughout to prove
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_jr^j<(1+r)^8.
\tag{1}
\]

Also, because \(w_i<p\), every singleton is losing, so
\[
d_0=1,\qquad d_1=9.
\]

We prove (1) separately on the three intervals
\[
\left[\frac16,\frac15\right],
\qquad
\left[\frac15,\frac14\right],
\qquad
\left[\frac14,\frac13\right).
\]

### 1. The interval \(\frac16\le p\le\frac15\)

Here
\[
\frac15\le r\le\frac14.
\]

No losing set has size at least \(5\). Indeed, if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge5\), then \(|A^c|\le4\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction.

Thus only layers \(0,1,2,3,4\) occur.

For losing pairs, four disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining vertex would have total weight
\[
<4p+p\le1,
\]
contradicting \(\sum_iw_i=1\). Hence the graph of losing pairs has matching number at most \(3\). By the Erdős–Gallai matching bound for graphs on \(9\) vertices,
\[
d_2\le21.
\]

For losing triples, three disjoint losing triples would cover all \(9\) vertices and have total weight at most \(3p<1\), impossible. Thus the losing triples contain no perfect matching. The standard finite matching bound for \(3\)-uniform hypergraphs on \(9\) vertices gives
\[
d_3\le56.
\]
Equivalently, one can see this by counting partitions of \([9]\) into three triples: there are \(280\) such partitions, each triple lies in \(10\) partitions, and a family with no full partition must miss at least \(28\) triples, so it has at most \(84-28=56\) triples.

For losing \(4\)-sets, two disjoint losing \(4\)-sets plus the remaining vertex would have total weight
\[
<2p+p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence \(D\cap\binom{[9]}4\) is intersecting, so by Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
d_4\le\binom83=56.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_j d_jr^j
\le
1+9r+21r^2+56r^3+56r^4.
\]
The exact rational certificate below verifies
\[
(1+r)^8-\left(1+9r+21r^2+56r^3+56r^4\right)>0
\]
for all \(r\in[1/5,1/4]\).

### 2. The interval \(\frac15\le p\le\frac14\)

Here
\[
\frac14\le r\le\frac13.
\]

No losing set has size at least \(6\), since if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge6\), then \(|A^c|\le3\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1,
\]
impossible.

Define the winning-pair graph
\[
W=\{ij: w_i+w_j>p\},
\]
and let
\[
m=|W|.
\]
Then
\[
d_2=36-m.
\]

Let \(H=D\cap\binom{[9]}3\), and put \(h=|H|=d_3\). If two losing triples \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint, then their complement \(C=[9]\setminus(T\cup U)\) has size \(3\). If \(C\) contained a losing pair \(e\), with leftover vertex \(z\), then
\[
1=w(T)+w(U)+w(e)+w_z<4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(C\) must be a triangle of \(W\).

Let \(I(H)\) be the number of unordered disjoint pairs in \(H\), and let \(\tau(W)\) be the number of triangles in \(W\). Each triangle \(C\) of \(W\) supports exactly \(10\) unordered partitions of the other \(6\) vertices into two triples. Hence
\[
I(H)\le10\tau(W).
\]
The Kneser graph \(KG(9,3)\), whose vertices are triples and whose edges join disjoint triples, has \(84\) vertices, degree \(20\), and least eigenvalue \(-10\). Hoffman’s supersaturation bound gives
\[
I(H)\ge\frac5{28}h(h-28).
\]
If \(T_m\) denotes the maximum number of triangles in a graph with \(m\) edges, then
\[
h(h-28)\le56T_m.
\]
The exact value of \(T_m\) is the Kruskal–Katona/colex value:
if
\[
m=\binom a2+b,\qquad 0\le b<a,
\]
then
\[
T_m=\binom a3+\binom b2.
\]
Together with \(h\le56\), define
\[
H_m=\max\{h\in\{0,1,\dots,56\}:h(h-28)\le56T_m\}.
\]
Then
\[
d_3\le H_m.
\]

For losing \(4\)-sets, let \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}4\), and set \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=5\). If \(B\) contained two disjoint losing pairs plus a leftover vertex, then together with \(A\) those sets would have total weight
\[
<4p\le1,
\]
impossible. Thus the losing-pair graph inside \(B\) has matching number at most \(1\), so it has at most \(4\) edges. Therefore \(W[B]\) has at least \(6\) edges. Counting incidences between winning edges and such \(5\)-sets \(B\),
\[
6d_4\le m\binom73=35m.
\]
Also the losing \(4\)-sets are intersecting, so \(d_4\le56\). Hence
\[
d_4\le A_m:=\min\left\{56,\left\lfloor\frac{35m}{6}\right\rfloor\right\}.
\]

For losing \(5\)-sets, if \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}5\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(4\). If \(B\) had a perfect matching of losing pairs, then
\[
1\le w(A)+p+p\le3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus \(W[B]\) meets every perfect matching of \(K_4\), which requires at least \(3\) winning edges. Counting incidences gives
\[
3d_5\le m\binom72=21m,
\]
so \(d_5\le7m\).

Moreover, complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\), because if two complements \(B,B'\) met in at most one vertex, then
\[
2q\le w(B)+w(B')<1+p,
\]
contradicting \(p\le1/4<1/3\). By the \(t=2\) Erdős–Ko–Rado bound,
\[
d_5\le21.
\]
Thus
\[
d_5\le B_m:=\min\{21,7m\}.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_jd_jr^j
\le
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5.
\]
The exact rational certificate below verifies positivity of
\[
(1+r)^8-
\left(
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5
\right)
\]
for every \(m=0,\dots,36\) and every \(r\in[1/4,1/3]\).

```python
# Exact certificate for the intervals [1/6,1/5] and [1/5,1/4].
# Uses only integer arithmetic and rational Sturm root counting.

import sympy as sp
from math import comb

r = sp.symbols("r")

def assert_positive_on_poly(P, lo, hi, tag):
    P = sp.Poly(sp.expand(P), r)
    assert P.eval(lo) > 0, ("left endpoint failed", tag, P.eval(lo))
    assert P.eval(hi) > 0, ("right endpoint failed", tag, P.eval(hi))
    assert P.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, ("root in interval", tag, P)

# Interval [1/6,1/5], i.e. r in [1/5,1/4].
P_low = (1 + r)**8 - (1 + 9*r + 21*r**2 + 56*r**3 + 56*r**4)
assert_positive_on_poly(P_low, sp.Rational(1, 5), sp.Rational(1, 4), "low interval")

# Interval [1/5,1/4], i.e. r in [1/4,1/3].
def tri_max(m: int) -> int:
    # Maximum number of triangles in an m-edge graph.
    a = 0
    while comb(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - comb(a, 2)
    return comb(a, 3) + comb(b, 2)

def H_bound(m: int) -> int:
    T = tri_max(m)
    best = 0
    for h in range(57):  # h <= 56
        if h * (h - 28) <= 56 * T:
            best = h
    return best

def A_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(56, (35 * m) // 6)

def B_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(21, 7 * m)

for m in range(37):
    H = H_bound(m)
    A = A_bound(m)
    B = B_bound(m)
    P_mid = (1 + r)**8 - (
        1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2 + H*r**3 + A*r**4 + B*r**5
    )
    assert_positive_on_poly(
        P_mid,
        sp.Rational(1, 4),
        sp.Rational(1, 3),
        ("middle interval", m, H, A, B),
    )

print("Lower and middle interval certificates passed.")
```

Because the code uses exact rational endpoints and `Poly.count_roots`, which is Sturm root counting over the rationals, the verification is exact: endpoint positivity plus absence of real roots on the interval implies strict positivity throughout.

### 3. The interval \(\frac14\le p<\frac13\)

Here
\[
\frac13\le r<\frac12.
\]

No losing set has size at least \(7\), because if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge7\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+2p<1,
\]
a contradiction.

Again let
\[
W=\{ij:w_i+w_j>p\},\qquad m=|W|.
\]
Then
\[
d_2=36-m.
\]

We use the following bounds.

#### 3.1. Triple layer

As before, losing triples have no perfect matching, so
\[
d_3\le56.
\]

#### 3.2. The \(D_4\) improvement

Every losing set is independent in \(W\): if \(A\in D\) contained an edge \(ij\in W\), then
\[
w(A)\ge w_i+w_j>p,
\]
contradiction.

Also \(D_4:=D\cap\binom{[9]}4\) is intersecting. Indeed, if \(A,A'\in D_4\) were disjoint, with leftover vertex \(z\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A')+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible.

Thus \(D_4\) is an intersecting family of independent \(4\)-sets in the winning-pair graph \(W\).

The graph \(W\) is a threshold graph. Indeed, for any induced subgraph, let \(x\) be a vertex of minimum weight and \(y\) one of maximum weight. If \(w_x+w_y>p\), then \(y\) is adjacent to every other vertex; if \(w_x+w_y\le p\), then \(x\) is isolated.

Exact enumeration of threshold graphs on \(9\) vertices gives the following upper bounds for the largest intersecting family of independent \(4\)-sets in a threshold graph with \(m\) edges:
\[
I_0=56,\quad I_1=50,\quad I_2=45,\quad I_3=41,\quad I_4=38,\quad I_5=36,
\]
and
\[
I_m=35\qquad(6\le m\le36).
\]
Therefore
\[
d_4\le I_m.
\]

We also need a weighted-degree bound. Let
\[
\mathcal C=\{A^c:A\in D_4\}\subseteq\binom{[9]}5.
\]
Since \(w(A)\le p\), each \(B\in\mathcal C\) satisfies
\[
w(B)\ge q.
\]
Also \(\mathcal C\) is \(2\)-intersecting. Let its vertex degrees be
\[
\Delta_1\ge\Delta_2\ge\cdots\ge\Delta_9.
\]
Each link of \(\mathcal C\) is an intersecting \(4\)-family on \(8\) vertices, so
\[
\Delta_i\le\frac12\binom84=35.
\]
Now
\[
q|\mathcal C|
\le
\sum_{B\in\mathcal C}w(B)
=
\sum_i w_i\Delta_i.
\]
Subject to \(0\le w_i\le p\) and \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the right-hand side is maximized by placing mass \(p\) on the three largest degrees and the remaining mass \(1-3p\) on the fourth largest degree. Therefore
\[
\sum_iw_i\Delta_i
\le
p(\Delta_1+\Delta_2+\Delta_3)+(1-3p)\Delta_4
\le35.
\]
Hence
\[
qd_4\le35,
\]
so
\[
d_4\le\lfloor 35/q\rfloor=\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor.
\]

Combining the two \(D_4\) bounds,
\[
d_4\le C_m(r):=\min\{I_m,\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor\}.
\]

#### 3.3. The \(D_5\) and \(D_6\) bounds

If \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}5\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(4\). If \(B\) contained a perfect matching of losing pairs, then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le3p<1,
\]
impossible. Therefore \(W[B]\) blocks all three perfect matchings of \(K_4\), requiring at least \(3\) winning edges.

Thus:
- if \(m\le2\), then \(d_5=0\);
- if \(m=3\), then \(B\) must contain all three winning edges, and there are at most \(6\) such \(4\)-sets;
- if \(m\ge4\), the complements of losing \(5\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting \(4\)-sets, so \(d_5\le21\).

Hence
\[
d_5\le B_m,
\]
where
\[
B_m=
\begin{cases}
0,&m\le2,\\
6,&m=3,\\
21,&m\ge4.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}6\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(3\). If some pair in \(B\) were losing, then together with \(A\) and the remaining vertex of \(B\) the total weight would be
\[
<3p<1,
\]
impossible. Therefore \(B\) is a triangle of \(W\). Hence
\[
d_6\le T_m.
\]
Also the complements of losing \(6\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting triples, so
\[
d_6\le7.
\]
Thus
\[
d_6\le G_m:=\min\{7,T_m\}.
\]

#### 3.4. Deficiency cascade

For \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}k\), define its normalized deficiency by
\[
\epsilon(A)=\frac{p-w(A)}p\ge0,
\]
and put
\[
E_k=\sum_{A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}k}\epsilon(A).
\]

For \(k\ge2\), if \(B\in D\cap\binom{[9]}{k+1}\), then all \(k\)-subsets of \(B\) are losing, and
\[
\sum_{\substack{A\subset B\\ |A|=k}}(p-w(A))
=(k+1)p-kw(B)
=p+k(p-w(B)).
\]
After dividing by \(p\), summing over \(B\), and using that each losing \(k\)-set is contained in at most \(9-k\) losing \((k+1)\)-sets, we obtain
\[
d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le(9-k)E_k.
\tag{2}
\]

For pairs,
\[
E_2
=
\frac1p\sum_{\substack{ij\\ w_i+w_j\le p}}(p-w_i-w_j).
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{i<j}(p-w_i-w_j)=36p-8,
\]
and each winning pair contributes more than \(-p\), we get
\[
E_2\le 28+m-\frac8r.
\tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) for \(k=2,3,4,5\) gives
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le7E_2.
\]
Using (3),
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le
196+7m-\frac{56}{r}.
\tag{4}
\]

#### 3.5. Exact LP/Sturm certificate

For fixed \(m\), the actual vector
\[
(x_3,x_4,x_5,x_6)=(d_3,d_4,d_5,d_6)
\]
satisfies
\[
0\le x_3\le56,
\]
\[
0\le x_4\le C_m(r),
\]
\[
0\le x_5\le B_m,
\]
\[
0\le x_6\le G_m,
\]
and the deficiency-budget constraint
\[
x_3+\frac{x_4}{3}+\frac{x_5+x_6}{5}
\le
196+7m-\frac{56}{r}.
\]

Thus
\[
\sum_jd_jr^j
=
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+d_3r^3+d_4r^4+d_5r^5+d_6r^6
\]
is bounded above by the optimum of this linear program.

The following code enumerates all LP vertices, checks all threshold-graph \(D_4\) bounds, and verifies by exact Sturm root counting that the LP optimum is always strictly below \((1+r)^8\) for every \(m=0,\dots,36\) and every \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\). This includes the desired half-open range \(r\in[1/3,1/2)\).

```python
# Exact certificate for the interval [1/4,1/3), i.e. r in [1/3,1/2).
# All arithmetic is exact over integers/rationals. Sturm root counting is used
# for every one-variable rational inequality.

import sympy as sp
from itertools import combinations, product
from functools import lru_cache
from math import comb

r = sp.symbols("r")

# ---------- Threshold-graph enumeration for the D4 bound ----------

def threshold_graphs(n=9):
    # A threshold graph is generated, up to isomorphism, by adding each new
    # vertex as isolated (0) or dominating (1). The graph invariant checked
    # below is isomorphism-invariant, so one representative per creation
    # sequence is enough.
    seen = set()
    for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=n - 1):
        adj = [0] * n
        for v, b in enumerate(bits, start=1):
            if b:
                for u in range(v):
                    adj[v] |= 1 << u
                    adj[u] |= 1 << v
        tup = tuple(adj)
        if tup not in seen:
            seen.add(tup)
            yield tup

def edge_count(adj):
    return sum(a.bit_count() for a in adj) // 2

def max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj):
    n = 9

    # List all independent 4-sets of the graph.
    sets = []
    for C in combinations(range(n), 4):
        ok = True
        for i, j in combinations(C, 2):
            if (adj[i] >> j) & 1:
                ok = False
                break
        if ok:
            mask = sum(1 << i for i in C)
            sets.append(mask)

    # Conflict graph: two independent 4-sets conflict if they are disjoint.
    # An intersecting family is an independent set in this conflict graph.
    N = len(sets)
    conflict = [0] * N
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if sets[i] & sets[j] == 0:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i

    @lru_cache(None)
    def mis(P):
        # Exact maximum independent set by the recurrence:
        # alpha(P)=max(alpha(P\v),1+alpha(P\v-N(v))).
        if P == 0:
            return 0

        # Include isolated conflict-vertices automatically.
        iso = 0
        Q = P
        while Q:
            lb = Q & -Q
            v = lb.bit_length() - 1
            if conflict[v] & P == 0:
                iso |= lb
            Q -= lb
        if iso:
            return iso.bit_count() + mis(P & ~iso)

        # Branch on a maximum-degree conflict vertex.
        best_v = None
        best_deg = -1
        Q = P
        while Q:
            lb = Q & -Q
            v = lb.bit_length() - 1
            deg = (conflict[v] & P).bit_count()
            if deg > best_deg:
                best_deg = deg
                best_v = v
            Q -= lb

        v = best_v
        without = mis(P & ~(1 << v))
        with_v = 1 + mis(P & ~(1 << v) & ~conflict[v])
        return max(without, with_v)

    return mis((1 << N) - 1)

I = [56, 50, 45, 41, 38, 36] + [35] * 31

max_by_m = [0] * 37
for adj in threshold_graphs(9):
    m = edge_count(adj)
    val = max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj)
    max_by_m[m] = max(max_by_m[m], val)

assert all(max_by_m[m] <= I[m] for m in range(37))


# ---------- Layer bounds ----------

def tri_max(m):
    # Maximum number of triangles in an m-edge graph.
    a = 0
    while comb(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - comb(a, 2)
    return comb(a, 3) + comb(b, 2)

def B_cap(m):
    if m <= 2:
        return 0
    if m == 3:
        return 6
    return 21

def G_cap(m):
    return min(7, tri_max(m))


# ---------- Exact positivity checking for rational functions ----------

def assert_positive_on(expr, lo, hi, tag):
    # Multiplication by r is safe since r>0 on [1/3,1/2].
    expr = sp.together(expr * r)
    num, den = sp.together(expr).as_numer_denom()
    num = sp.expand(num)
    den = sp.expand(den)

    if lo == hi:
        assert sp.simplify(num.subs(r, lo)) > 0, tag
        assert sp.simplify(den.subs(r, lo)) > 0, tag
        return

    den_poly = sp.Poly(den, r)
    assert den_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and den_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert den_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

    num_poly = sp.Poly(num, r)
    assert num_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and num_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert num_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

def intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r >= 0, with r>0.
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return None
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    lo2 = max(lo, root)
    if lo2 > hi:
        return None
    return lo2, hi

def intersect_le_L(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r <= 0, with r>0.
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return lo, hi
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    hi2 = min(hi, root)
    if lo > hi2:
        return None
    return lo, hi2


# Breakpoints for the floor bound floor(35(1+r)).
# On each interval the code uses the right endpoint as a safe cap.
breaks = [
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(12, 35),
    sp.Rational(13, 35),
    sp.Rational(14, 35),
    sp.Rational(15, 35),
    sp.Rational(16, 35),
    sp.Rational(17, 35),
    sp.Rational(1, 2),
]

target = (1 + r) ** 8

# Variables are x3,x4,x5,x6.
cost = [
    sp.Rational(1, 1),
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
]
value = [r**3, r**4, r**5, r**6]

for m in range(37):
    for lo, hi in zip(breaks[:-1], breaks[1:]):
        K = int(sp.floor(35 * (1 + hi)))  # safe cap on this interval
        C4 = min(I[m], K)

        caps = [56, C4, B_cap(m), G_cap(m)]
        base = 1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2
        Lambda0 = sp.Rational(196 + 7*m)

        # Vertices with all variables fixed at bounds.
        for mask in range(16):
            xs = []
            fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)
            for i in range(4):
                xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if ((mask >> i) & 1) else sp.Rational(0)
                xs.append(xi)
                fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

            iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
            if iv is not None:
                a, b = iv
                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("fixed", m, lo, hi, mask))

        # Vertices with one free variable and the budget constraint active.
        for free in range(4):
            others = [i for i in range(4) if i != free]
            for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=3):
                xs = [None] * 4
                fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)
                for bit, i in zip(bits, others):
                    xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if bit else sp.Rational(0)
                    xs[i] = xi
                    fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

                # Need x_free >= 0.
                iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                # Need x_free <= cap.
                iv = intersect_le_L(
                    a,
                    b,
                    Lambda0 - fixed_cost - cost[free] * sp.Rational(caps[free])
                )
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                Lambda_expr = Lambda0 - sp.Rational(56, 1) / r
                xfree = (Lambda_expr - fixed_cost) / cost[free]
                xs[free] = xfree

                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("free", m, lo, hi, free, bits))

print("Upper interval certificate passed.")
```

The LP feasible region is a box cut by one halfspace, so every optimum occurs at one of the enumerated vertices: either all variables are at bounds, or the budget constraint is active and three variables are at bounds. The code checks the gap \((1+r)^8-\text{objective}\) at every such vertex. Since every checked gap is strictly positive, the actual layer polynomial is strictly smaller than \((1+r)^8\).

Therefore (1) holds on \([1/3,1/2)\), equivalently on \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\).

Combining the three intervals, for every
\[
\frac16\le p<\frac13
\]
we have
\[
\sum_jd_jr^j<(1+r)^8.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_iw_iv_i\le p\right]
=q^9\sum_jd_jr^j
<q^9(1+r)^8
=q.
\]
Thus
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_iw_iv_i>p\right]>1-q=p.
\]
This proves the stated support-\(9\) theorem.  

```json
{
  "original_problem": "Let $w_1, \\ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let \n$v_1, \\ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that\n\\[\\Pr\\Big[\\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\\geq p\\Big]\\geq p?\\]",
  "problem_solved": "Let p be a real number with 1/6 <= p < 1/3. Let w_1,...,w_9 be positive real numbers such that w_i < p for every i and sum_{i=1}^9 w_i = 1. Let v_1,...,v_9 be independent Bernoulli(p) random variables, meaning P(v_i=1)=p and P(v_i=0)=1-p. Then P[sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i > p] > p. This is a support-9 strict-reduced special case of the original problem, not the full original problem.",
  "is_relaxation": true
}
```
solution:
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

This proof establishes the following support-$9$ strict-reduced theorem.

Let \(p\) be a real number with
\[
\frac16\le p<\frac13.
\]
Let \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) be positive real numbers such that
\[
w_i<p\quad\text{for every }i\in\{1,\dots,9\},
\qquad
\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i=1.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_9\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, i.e.
\[
\Pr[v_i=1]=p,\qquad \Pr[v_i=0]=1-p.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

This is not the full original problem: it proves a strict support-\(9\) special case, with the additional hypotheses \(m=9\), \(0<w_i<p\), and with the strict event \(>p\).

## Proof

Let
\[
q=1-p,\qquad r=\frac pq.
\]
For \(A\subseteq[9]:=\{1,\dots,9\}\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]
Define the losing family
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[9]: w(A)\le p\},
\]
and its layer counts
\[
d_j=\left|D\cap\binom{[9]}j\right|.
\]

Let \(V=\{i:v_i=1\}\). Then
\[
\Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{9-|A|}
=q^9 r^{|A|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=\sum_{A\in D}p^{|A|}q^{9-|A|}
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j.
\]
Thus
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]>p
\]
is equivalent to
\[
q^9\sum_{j=0}^9d_jr^j<q.
\]
Since \(1+r=1/q\), we have
\[
q^9(1+r)^8=q.
\]
Hence it suffices throughout to prove
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_jr^j<(1+r)^8.
\tag{1}
\]

Also, because \(w_i<p\), every singleton is losing, so
\[
d_0=1,\qquad d_1=9.
\]

We prove (1) separately on the three intervals
\[
\left[\frac16,\frac15\right],
\qquad
\left[\frac15,\frac14\right],
\qquad
\left[\frac14,\frac13\right).
\]

### 1. The interval \(\frac16\le p\le\frac15\)

Here
\[
\frac15\le r\le\frac14.
\]

No losing set has size at least \(5\). Indeed, if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge5\), then \(|A^c|\le4\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction.

Thus only layers \(0,1,2,3,4\) occur.

For losing pairs, four disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining vertex would have total weight
\[
<4p+p\le1,
\]
contradicting \(\sum_iw_i=1\). Hence the graph of losing pairs has matching number at most \(3\). By the Erdős–Gallai matching bound for graphs on \(9\) vertices,
\[
d_2\le21.
\]

For losing triples, three disjoint losing triples would cover all \(9\) vertices and have total weight at most \(3p<1\), impossible. Thus the losing triples contain no perfect matching. The standard finite matching bound for \(3\)-uniform hypergraphs on \(9\) vertices gives
\[
d_3\le56.
\]
Equivalently, one can see this by counting partitions of \([9]\) into three triples: there are \(280\) such partitions, each triple lies in \(10\) partitions, and a family with no full partition must miss at least \(28\) triples, so it has at most \(84-28=56\) triples.

For losing \(4\)-sets, two disjoint losing \(4\)-sets plus the remaining vertex would have total weight
\[
<2p+p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence \(D\cap\binom{[9]}4\) is intersecting, so by Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
d_4\le\binom83=56.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_j d_jr^j
\le
1+9r+21r^2+56r^3+56r^4.
\]
The exact rational certificate below verifies
\[
(1+r)^8-\left(1+9r+21r^2+56r^3+56r^4\right)>0
\]
for all \(r\in[1/5,1/4]\).

### 2. The interval \(\frac15\le p\le\frac14\)

Here
\[
\frac14\le r\le\frac13.
\]

No losing set has size at least \(6\), since if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge6\), then \(|A^c|\le3\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1,
\]
impossible.

Define the winning-pair graph
\[
W=\{ij: w_i+w_j>p\},
\]
and let
\[
m=|W|.
\]
Then
\[
d_2=36-m.
\]

Let \(H=D\cap\binom{[9]}3\), and put \(h=|H|=d_3\). If two losing triples \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint, then their complement \(C=[9]\setminus(T\cup U)\) has size \(3\). If \(C\) contained a losing pair \(e\), with leftover vertex \(z\), then
\[
1=w(T)+w(U)+w(e)+w_z<4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(C\) must be a triangle of \(W\).

Let \(I(H)\) be the number of unordered disjoint pairs in \(H\), and let \(\tau(W)\) be the number of triangles in \(W\). Each triangle \(C\) of \(W\) supports exactly \(10\) unordered partitions of the other \(6\) vertices into two triples. Hence
\[
I(H)\le10\tau(W).
\]
The Kneser graph \(KG(9,3)\), whose vertices are triples and whose edges join disjoint triples, has \(84\) vertices, degree \(20\), and least eigenvalue \(-10\). Hoffman’s supersaturation bound gives
\[
I(H)\ge\frac5{28}h(h-28).
\]
If \(T_m\) denotes the maximum number of triangles in a graph with \(m\) edges, then
\[
h(h-28)\le56T_m.
\]
The exact value of \(T_m\) is the Kruskal–Katona/colex value:
if
\[
m=\binom a2+b,\qquad 0\le b<a,
\]
then
\[
T_m=\binom a3+\binom b2.
\]
Together with \(h\le56\), define
\[
H_m=\max\{h\in\{0,1,\dots,56\}:h(h-28)\le56T_m\}.
\]
Then
\[
d_3\le H_m.
\]

For losing \(4\)-sets, let \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}4\), and set \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=5\). If \(B\) contained two disjoint losing pairs plus a leftover vertex, then together with \(A\) those sets would have total weight
\[
<4p\le1,
\]
impossible. Thus the losing-pair graph inside \(B\) has matching number at most \(1\), so it has at most \(4\) edges. Therefore \(W[B]\) has at least \(6\) edges. Counting incidences between winning edges and such \(5\)-sets \(B\),
\[
6d_4\le m\binom73=35m.
\]
Also the losing \(4\)-sets are intersecting, so \(d_4\le56\). Hence
\[
d_4\le A_m:=\min\left\{56,\left\lfloor\frac{35m}{6}\right\rfloor\right\}.
\]

For losing \(5\)-sets, if \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}5\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(4\). If \(B\) had a perfect matching of losing pairs, then
\[
1\le w(A)+p+p\le3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus \(W[B]\) meets every perfect matching of \(K_4\), which requires at least \(3\) winning edges. Counting incidences gives
\[
3d_5\le m\binom72=21m,
\]
so \(d_5\le7m\).

Moreover, complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\), because if two complements \(B,B'\) met in at most one vertex, then
\[
2q\le w(B)+w(B')<1+p,
\]
contradicting \(p\le1/4<1/3\). By the \(t=2\) Erdős–Ko–Rado bound,
\[
d_5\le21.
\]
Thus
\[
d_5\le B_m:=\min\{21,7m\}.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_jd_jr^j
\le
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5.
\]
The exact rational certificate below verifies positivity of
\[
(1+r)^8-
\left(
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5
\right)
\]
for every \(m=0,\dots,36\) and every \(r\in[1/4,1/3]\).

```python
# Exact certificate for the intervals [1/6,1/5] and [1/5,1/4].
# Uses only integer arithmetic and rational Sturm root counting.

import sympy as sp
from math import comb

r = sp.symbols("r")

def assert_positive_on_poly(P, lo, hi, tag):
    P = sp.Poly(sp.expand(P), r)
    assert P.eval(lo) > 0, ("left endpoint failed", tag, P.eval(lo))
    assert P.eval(hi) > 0, ("right endpoint failed", tag, P.eval(hi))
    assert P.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, ("root in interval", tag, P)

# Interval [1/6,1/5], i.e. r in [1/5,1/4].
P_low = (1 + r)**8 - (1 + 9*r + 21*r**2 + 56*r**3 + 56*r**4)
assert_positive_on_poly(P_low, sp.Rational(1, 5), sp.Rational(1, 4), "low interval")

# Interval [1/5,1/4], i.e. r in [1/4,1/3].
def tri_max(m: int) -> int:
    # Maximum number of triangles in an m-edge graph.
    a = 0
    while comb(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - comb(a, 2)
    return comb(a, 3) + comb(b, 2)

def H_bound(m: int) -> int:
    T = tri_max(m)
    best = 0
    for h in range(57):  # h <= 56
        if h * (h - 28) <= 56 * T:
            best = h
    return best

def A_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(56, (35 * m) // 6)

def B_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(21, 7 * m)

for m in range(37):
    H = H_bound(m)
    A = A_bound(m)
    B = B_bound(m)
    P_mid = (1 + r)**8 - (
        1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2 + H*r**3 + A*r**4 + B*r**5
    )
    assert_positive_on_poly(
        P_mid,
        sp.Rational(1, 4),
        sp.Rational(1, 3),
        ("middle interval", m, H, A, B),
    )

print("Lower and middle interval certificates passed.")
```

Because the code uses exact rational endpoints and `Poly.count_roots`, which is Sturm root counting over the rationals, the verification is exact: endpoint positivity plus absence of real roots on the interval implies strict positivity throughout.

### 3. The interval \(\frac14\le p<\frac13\)

Here
\[
\frac13\le r<\frac12.
\]

No losing set has size at least \(7\), because if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge7\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+2p<1,
\]
a contradiction.

Again let
\[
W=\{ij:w_i+w_j>p\},\qquad m=|W|.
\]
Then
\[
d_2=36-m.
\]

We use the following bounds.

#### 3.1. Triple layer

As before, losing triples have no perfect matching, so
\[
d_3\le56.
\]

#### 3.2. The \(D_4\) improvement

Every losing set is independent in \(W\): if \(A\in D\) contained an edge \(ij\in W\), then
\[
w(A)\ge w_i+w_j>p,
\]
contradiction.

Also \(D_4:=D\cap\binom{[9]}4\) is intersecting. Indeed, if \(A,A'\in D_4\) were disjoint, with leftover vertex \(z\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A')+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible.

Thus \(D_4\) is an intersecting family of independent \(4\)-sets in the winning-pair graph \(W\).

The graph \(W\) is a threshold graph. Indeed, for any induced subgraph, let \(x\) be a vertex of minimum weight and \(y\) one of maximum weight. If \(w_x+w_y>p\), then \(y\) is adjacent to every other vertex; if \(w_x+w_y\le p\), then \(x\) is isolated.

Exact enumeration of threshold graphs on \(9\) vertices gives the following upper bounds for the largest intersecting family of independent \(4\)-sets in a threshold graph with \(m\) edges:
\[
I_0=56,\quad I_1=50,\quad I_2=45,\quad I_3=41,\quad I_4=38,\quad I_5=36,
\]
and
\[
I_m=35\qquad(6\le m\le36).
\]
Therefore
\[
d_4\le I_m.
\]

We also need a weighted-degree bound. Let
\[
\mathcal C=\{A^c:A\in D_4\}\subseteq\binom{[9]}5.
\]
Since \(w(A)\le p\), each \(B\in\mathcal C\) satisfies
\[
w(B)\ge q.
\]
Also \(\mathcal C\) is \(2\)-intersecting. Let its vertex degrees be
\[
\Delta_1\ge\Delta_2\ge\cdots\ge\Delta_9.
\]
Each link of \(\mathcal C\) is an intersecting \(4\)-family on \(8\) vertices, so
\[
\Delta_i\le\frac12\binom84=35.
\]
Now
\[
q|\mathcal C|
\le
\sum_{B\in\mathcal C}w(B)
=
\sum_i w_i\Delta_i.
\]
Subject to \(0\le w_i\le p\) and \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the right-hand side is maximized by placing mass \(p\) on the three largest degrees and the remaining mass \(1-3p\) on the fourth largest degree. Therefore
\[
\sum_iw_i\Delta_i
\le
p(\Delta_1+\Delta_2+\Delta_3)+(1-3p)\Delta_4
\le35.
\]
Hence
\[
qd_4\le35,
\]
so
\[
d_4\le\lfloor 35/q\rfloor=\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor.
\]

Combining the two \(D_4\) bounds,
\[
d_4\le C_m(r):=\min\{I_m,\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor\}.
\]

#### 3.3. The \(D_5\) and \(D_6\) bounds

If \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}5\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(4\). If \(B\) contained a perfect matching of losing pairs, then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le3p<1,
\]
impossible. Therefore \(W[B]\) blocks all three perfect matchings of \(K_4\), requiring at least \(3\) winning edges.

Thus:
- if \(m\le2\), then \(d_5=0\);
- if \(m=3\), then \(B\) must contain all three winning edges, and there are at most \(6\) such \(4\)-sets;
- if \(m\ge4\), the complements of losing \(5\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting \(4\)-sets, so \(d_5\le21\).

Hence
\[
d_5\le B_m,
\]
where
\[
B_m=
\begin{cases}
0,&m\le2,\\
6,&m=3,\\
21,&m\ge4.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}6\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(3\). If some pair in \(B\) were losing, then together with \(A\) and the remaining vertex of \(B\) the total weight would be
\[
<3p<1,
\]
impossible. Therefore \(B\) is a triangle of \(W\). Hence
\[
d_6\le T_m.
\]
Also the complements of losing \(6\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting triples, so
\[
d_6\le7.
\]
Thus
\[
d_6\le G_m:=\min\{7,T_m\}.
\]

#### 3.4. Deficiency cascade

For \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}k\), define its normalized deficiency by
\[
\epsilon(A)=\frac{p-w(A)}p\ge0,
\]
and put
\[
E_k=\sum_{A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}k}\epsilon(A).
\]

For \(k\ge2\), if \(B\in D\cap\binom{[9]}{k+1}\), then all \(k\)-subsets of \(B\) are losing, and
\[
\sum_{\substack{A\subset B\\ |A|=k}}(p-w(A))
=(k+1)p-kw(B)
=p+k(p-w(B)).
\]
After dividing by \(p\), summing over \(B\), and using that each losing \(k\)-set is contained in at most \(9-k\) losing \((k+1)\)-sets, we obtain
\[
d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le(9-k)E_k.
\tag{2}
\]

For pairs,
\[
E_2
=
\frac1p\sum_{\substack{ij\\ w_i+w_j\le p}}(p-w_i-w_j).
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{i<j}(p-w_i-w_j)=36p-8,
\]
and each winning pair contributes more than \(-p\), we get
\[
E_2\le 28+m-\frac8r.
\tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) for \(k=2,3,4,5\) gives
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le7E_2.
\]
Using (3),
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le
196+7m-\frac{56}{r}.
\tag{4}
\]

#### 3.5. Exact LP/Sturm certificate

For fixed \(m\), the actual vector
\[
(x_3,x_4,x_5,x_6)=(d_3,d_4,d_5,d_6)
\]
satisfies
\[
0\le x_3\le56,
\]
\[
0\le x_4\le C_m(r),
\]
\[
0\le x_5\le B_m,
\]
\[
0\le x_6\le G_m,
\]
and the deficiency-budget constraint
\[
x_3+\frac{x_4}{3}+\frac{x_5+x_6}{5}
\le
196+7m-\frac{56}{r}.
\]

Thus
\[
\sum_jd_jr^j
=
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+d_3r^3+d_4r^4+d_5r^5+d_6r^6
\]
is bounded above by the optimum of this linear program.

The following code enumerates all LP vertices, checks all threshold-graph \(D_4\) bounds, and verifies by exact Sturm root counting that the LP optimum is always strictly below \((1+r)^8\) for every \(m=0,\dots,36\) and every \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\). This includes the desired half-open range \(r\in[1/3,1/2)\).

```python
# Exact certificate for the interval [1/4,1/3), i.e. r in [1/3,1/2).
# All arithmetic is exact over integers/rationals. Sturm root counting is used
# for every one-variable rational inequality.

import sympy as sp
from itertools import combinations, product
from functools import lru_cache
from math import comb

r = sp.symbols("r")

# ---------- Threshold-graph enumeration for the D4 bound ----------

def threshold_graphs(n=9):
    # A threshold graph is generated, up to isomorphism, by adding each new
    # vertex as isolated (0) or dominating (1). The graph invariant checked
    # below is isomorphism-invariant, so one representative per creation
    # sequence is enough.
    seen = set()
    for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=n - 1):
        adj = [0] * n
        for v, b in enumerate(bits, start=1):
            if b:
                for u in range(v):
                    adj[v] |= 1 << u
                    adj[u] |= 1 << v
        tup = tuple(adj)
        if tup not in seen:
            seen.add(tup)
            yield tup

def edge_count(adj):
    return sum(a.bit_count() for a in adj) // 2

def max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj):
    n = 9

    # List all independent 4-sets of the graph.
    sets = []
    for C in combinations(range(n), 4):
        ok = True
        for i, j in combinations(C, 2):
            if (adj[i] >> j) & 1:
                ok = False
                break
        if ok:
            mask = sum(1 << i for i in C)
            sets.append(mask)

    # Conflict graph: two independent 4-sets conflict if they are disjoint.
    # An intersecting family is an independent set in this conflict graph.
    N = len(sets)
    conflict = [0] * N
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if sets[i] & sets[j] == 0:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i

    @lru_cache(None)
    def mis(P):
        # Exact maximum independent set by the recurrence:
        # alpha(P)=max(alpha(P\v),1+alpha(P\v-N(v))).
        if P == 0:
            return 0

        # Include isolated conflict-vertices automatically.
        iso = 0
        Q = P
        while Q:
            lb = Q & -Q
            v = lb.bit_length() - 1
            if conflict[v] & P == 0:
                iso |= lb
            Q -= lb
        if iso:
            return iso.bit_count() + mis(P & ~iso)

        # Branch on a maximum-degree conflict vertex.
        best_v = None
        best_deg = -1
        Q = P
        while Q:
            lb = Q & -Q
            v = lb.bit_length() - 1
            deg = (conflict[v] & P).bit_count()
            if deg > best_deg:
                best_deg = deg
                best_v = v
            Q -= lb

        v = best_v
        without = mis(P & ~(1 << v))
        with_v = 1 + mis(P & ~(1 << v) & ~conflict[v])
        return max(without, with_v)

    return mis((1 << N) - 1)

I = [56, 50, 45, 41, 38, 36] + [35] * 31

max_by_m = [0] * 37
for adj in threshold_graphs(9):
    m = edge_count(adj)
    val = max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj)
    max_by_m[m] = max(max_by_m[m], val)

assert all(max_by_m[m] <= I[m] for m in range(37))


# ---------- Layer bounds ----------

def tri_max(m):
    # Maximum number of triangles in an m-edge graph.
    a = 0
    while comb(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - comb(a, 2)
    return comb(a, 3) + comb(b, 2)

def B_cap(m):
    if m <= 2:
        return 0
    if m == 3:
        return 6
    return 21

def G_cap(m):
    return min(7, tri_max(m))


# ---------- Exact positivity checking for rational functions ----------

def assert_positive_on(expr, lo, hi, tag):
    # Multiplication by r is safe since r>0 on [1/3,1/2].
    expr = sp.together(expr * r)
    num, den = sp.together(expr).as_numer_denom()
    num = sp.expand(num)
    den = sp.expand(den)

    if lo == hi:
        assert sp.simplify(num.subs(r, lo)) > 0, tag
        assert sp.simplify(den.subs(r, lo)) > 0, tag
        return

    den_poly = sp.Poly(den, r)
    assert den_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and den_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert den_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

    num_poly = sp.Poly(num, r)
    assert num_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and num_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert num_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

def intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r >= 0, with r>0.
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return None
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    lo2 = max(lo, root)
    if lo2 > hi:
        return None
    return lo2, hi

def intersect_le_L(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r <= 0, with r>0.
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return lo, hi
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    hi2 = min(hi, root)
    if lo > hi2:
        return None
    return lo, hi2


# Breakpoints for the floor bound floor(35(1+r)).
# On each interval the code uses the right endpoint as a safe cap.
breaks = [
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(12, 35),
    sp.Rational(13, 35),
    sp.Rational(14, 35),
    sp.Rational(15, 35),
    sp.Rational(16, 35),
    sp.Rational(17, 35),
    sp.Rational(1, 2),
]

target = (1 + r) ** 8

# Variables are x3,x4,x5,x6.
cost = [
    sp.Rational(1, 1),
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
]
value = [r**3, r**4, r**5, r**6]

for m in range(37):
    for lo, hi in zip(breaks[:-1], breaks[1:]):
        K = int(sp.floor(35 * (1 + hi)))  # safe cap on this interval
        C4 = min(I[m], K)

        caps = [56, C4, B_cap(m), G_cap(m)]
        base = 1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2
        Lambda0 = sp.Rational(196 + 7*m)

        # Vertices with all variables fixed at bounds.
        for mask in range(16):
            xs = []
            fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)
            for i in range(4):
                xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if ((mask >> i) & 1) else sp.Rational(0)
                xs.append(xi)
                fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

            iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
            if iv is not None:
                a, b = iv
                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("fixed", m, lo, hi, mask))

        # Vertices with one free variable and the budget constraint active.
        for free in range(4):
            others = [i for i in range(4) if i != free]
            for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=3):
                xs = [None] * 4
                fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)
                for bit, i in zip(bits, others):
                    xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if bit else sp.Rational(0)
                    xs[i] = xi
                    fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

                # Need x_free >= 0.
                iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                # Need x_free <= cap.
                iv = intersect_le_L(
                    a,
                    b,
                    Lambda0 - fixed_cost - cost[free] * sp.Rational(caps[free])
                )
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                Lambda_expr = Lambda0 - sp.Rational(56, 1) / r
                xfree = (Lambda_expr - fixed_cost) / cost[free]
                xs[free] = xfree

                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("free", m, lo, hi, free, bits))

print("Upper interval certificate passed.")
```

The LP feasible region is a box cut by one halfspace, so every optimum occurs at one of the enumerated vertices: either all variables are at bounds, or the budget constraint is active and three variables are at bounds. The code checks the gap \((1+r)^8-\text{objective}\) at every such vertex. Since every checked gap is strictly positive, the actual layer polynomial is strictly smaller than \((1+r)^8\).

Therefore (1) holds on \([1/3,1/2)\), equivalently on \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\).

Combining the three intervals, for every
\[
\frac16\le p<\frac13
\]
we have
\[
\sum_jd_jr^j<(1+r)^8.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_iw_iv_i\le p\right]
=q^9\sum_jd_jr^j
<q^9(1+r)^8
=q.
\]
Thus
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_iw_iv_i>p\right]>1-q=p.
\]
This proves the stated support-\(9\) theorem.
self_declared_problem_solved: Let p be a real number with 1/6 <= p < 1/3. Let w_1,...,w_9 be positive real numbers such that w_i < p for every i and sum_{i=1}^9 w_i = 1. Let v_1,...,v_9 be independent Bernoulli(p) random variables, meaning P(v_i=1)=p and P(v_i=0)=1-p. Then P[sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i > p] > p. This is a support-9 strict-reduced special case of the original problem, not the full original problem.
advisor_statement: Strict reduced support-9 theorem on the base interval: for every p in [1/6,1/3), every nine positive weights 0p]>p for iid Bernoulli(p) variables.

description: Attack the asymmetric one-coordinate tail-ratio inequality that could replace absolute Berry-Esseen.
full_text: ## 1. Why the lemma would prove the original theorem Use the **non-strict** convention in the original problem. Let \[ S=a v+T,\qquad v\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(p),\qquad T=\sum_i x_i\xi_i, \] with \(v\) independent of \(T\), \(0p]\), the matching identity is \[ \Pr[S>p]-p = q\,\Pr[T>p]-p\,\Pr[T\le p-a]. \] --- ## 2. A full proof at reciprocal points \(p=1/k\) The lemma is true for every \[ p=\frac1k,\qquad k\ge 2. \] This includes the important endpoint \(p=1/3\). The proof is the relative-tail version of the standard cyclic/coloring proof for \(p=1/k\). ([math.stackexchange.com](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/265163/convex-combinations-of-low-probability-bernoulli-variables)) Let \(p=1/k\). Color each tail coordinate independently and uniformly with one of \(k\) colors. For a fixed coloring, define \[ T_r=\sum_{i:\,{\rm color}(i)=r}x_i,\qquad r=1,\dots,k. \] For each fixed \(r\), \(T_r\) has the same distribution as \(T\). For this fixed coloring, let \[ U=\#\{r:T_r\ge 1/k\},\qquad L=\#\{r:T_r<1/k-a\}. \] I claim pointwise that \[ L\le (k-1)U. \] Indeed: - If \(U=0\), then every \(T_r<1/k\). If also \(L\ge1\), then for some color \(r_0\), \[ T_{r_0}<1/k-a, \] and all other \(k-1\) color classes have mass \(<1/k\). Hence \[ \sum_{r=1}^k T_r < (1/k-a)+(k-1)(1/k) = 1-a, \] contradicting \(\sum_r T_r=\sum_i x_i=1-a\). Thus \(U=0\Rightarrow L=0\). - If \(U\ge1\), then trivially \(L\le k-U\), and \[ k-U\le (k-1)U \] for every \(U\ge1\). Taking expectations and using symmetry, \[ \mathbb E U=k\Pr[T\ge 1/k],\qquad \mathbb E L=k\Pr[T<1/k-a]. \] Thus \[ \Pr[T<1/k-a]\le (k-1)\Pr[T\ge 1/k], \] which is exactly \[ q\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\Pr[T1-p=q\ge 2p, \] two weights both \(q\ge2p\), every pair has weight \[ B-x_i>B-p>1-2p\ge p. \] Thus \(T\ge p\) occurs whenever at least two of the three tail Bernoulli variables are selected: \[ \Pr[T\ge p]=3p^2q+p^3=p^2(3-2p). \] Next, no singleton can lie below \(p-a\). If, say, \(x_i1-a>1-p\), hence \(p>1/4\). On \([1/4,1/3]\), \[ 5p-3p^2-1>0. \] So the lemma holds for tail support \(\le3\). --- ## 4. Regular-tail certificate: the exact relative Berry–Esseen form Let \[ X=T-p(1-a),\qquad \sigma^2=pq\sum_i x_i^2, \] and define \[ \rho=\frac{\sum_i x_i^3}{\left(\sum_i x_i^2\right)^{3/2}}. \] Berry–Esseen gives, with \[ K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}, \qquad \delta=C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho, \] the bounds \[ \Pr[T\ge p] = \Pr[X\ge pa] \ge \overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{pa}{\sigma}\right)-\delta, \] and \[ \Pr[Tp\) and \(pa\le qa\). This is the clean relative-tail certificate. Its weakness is exactly the one noted in the earlier work: for far-tail regimes \(a/\sigma\gg1\), the Gaussian margin can be exponentially small, while the absolute Berry–Esseen error is only polynomial in the regularity parameter. --- ## 5. The lemma is false immediately above \(1/3\) This does **not** refute the asked range \(p\le1/3\), but it shows the \(1/3\) boundary is real. Similar three-weight counterexamples above \(1/3\) appear in the original Math.SE discussion. ([math.stackexchange.com](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/265163/convex-combinations-of-low-probability-bernoulli-variables)) Let \[ \frac131\), this becomes a robust Manickam–Miklós–Singhi-type counting problem. --- ## Current conclusion I do **not** have a counterexample for \(p\le1/3\). The lemma is true in several decisive regimes: \(p=1/k\), tail atom \(\ge p\), tail support \(\le3\), and regular tails satisfying the explicit relative Berry–Esseen margin. self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

description: Use the quantitative merge/Fourier constraints to rule out minimum-support counterexamples with support at least 10.
full_text:
I did not close the whole large-support case, but there is a stronger reduction than the bottom-5 one: **minimality forces every pair, not just mergeable bottom pairs, to have a positive level-2 coefficient of size at least \(\epsilon\)**. This yields much sharper quantitative obstructions. Let \(f=1_G=1_{\{S\ge p\}}\), \(\alpha=\mu_p(G)=p-\epsilon\), and \(\chi_i=(v_i-p)/\sqrt{pq}\). ## 1. Minimality makes **all** pairs bad For any pair \(i,j\), let \(a=\max(w_i,w_j)\), \(b=\min(w_i,w_j)\), and let \[ R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell . \] After merging \(w_i,w_j\) into \(w_i+w_j\), the new instance is not a counterexample: if \(w_i+w_j { "proven_results": [ { "statement": "In a minimum-support strict reduced counterexample, every pair of coordinates, not only mergeable bottom pairs, satisfies the merge gap inequality pq(L-U)>=epsilon, equivalently the p-biased level-2 Fourier coefficient of 1_G on that pair is at least epsilon.", "is_original_problem": false, "sketch": "After merging any pair, either the new weight is

=p and the merged instance trivially has success probability at least p. The exact pair-merge formula then gives the coefficient lower bound." }, { "statement": "For every coordinate i, epsilon(1-w_i)<=p w_i I_i, where I_i is the pivotal probability; consequently w_i>=epsilon/p and epsilon<=p/n.", "is_original_problem": false, "sketch": "Sum the positive pair covariances against the outside weights and use Cov(1_{A_i},R_i)<=p w_i I_i. Also A_i with v_i=1 lies inside G, so pI_i<=p-epsilon." }, { "statement": "Any such counterexample satisfies epsilon^2[(q/p) sum_i ((1-w_i)/w_i)^2 + binom(n,2)] <= (p-epsilon)(q+epsilon), hence epsilon < 1/(3(n-1)sqrt(n)) for p in [1/4,1/3].", "is_original_problem": false, "sketch": "Use the influence lower bounds to lower-bound first-level Fourier mass and use the all-pair coefficient lower bound to lower-bound second-level Fourier mass; Parseval bounds their sum by Var(1_G). Jensen minimizes the reciprocal-weight term at equal weights." }, { "statement": "The pivotal family of the smallest coordinate is an antichain, giving epsilon <= p M_{n-1}(p)/(n-1), where M_N(p) is the largest binomial atom. In particular, for n=10 and p in [1/4,1/3], epsilon<0.0102.", "is_original_problem": false, "sketch": "The pivotal interval for w_1 has length w_1 and all other weights are at least w_1, so two comparable sets cannot both be pivotal. LYM bounds its p-biased measure by the largest binomial layer, which combines with epsilon(1-w_1)<=p w_1 I_1." } ], "failed_attempts": [ { "approach": "Bottom-pair Parseval only", "reason": "Using only the six bottom-four or nine bottom-five coefficients gives epsilon bounds around 0.1, far too weak." }, { "approach": "Weighted influence identity alone", "reason": "It yields epsilon<=p/n after the all-pair upgrade, but does not force epsilon=0." }, { "approach": "Smallest-coordinate antichain alone", "reason": "It gives a sharp O(n^{-3/2}) gap reduction but still permits arbitrarily small positive epsilon." } ], "progress_notes": "The remaining obstruction is now a tiny-gap complete-positive-level-2 LTF: every pair merge must be bad, every weight is at least epsilon/p, and for support 10 the counterexample gap would have to be below about 0.0102. A full contradiction would follow by ruling out complete pair-positivity for mean-threshold LTFs in this range or by proving a support-9 merge-stability margin at this scale." }

A full contradiction would follow by ruling out complete pair-positivity for mean-threshold LTFs in this range or by proving a support-9 merge-stability margin at this scale." }

solution:
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

This write-up proves the following partial and conditional results, not the full characterization asked for in the original problem.

Let \(p\in(0,1)\), let \(q=1-p\), let \(m\ge1\), let \(w_1,\dots,w_m\ge0\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), let \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, meaning \(\Pr[v_i=1]=p\) and \(\Pr[v_i=0]=q\), and define
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i,\qquad \mu=\Pr[S<p].
\]
Since \(\Pr[S\ge p]=1-\mu\), the desired inequality is equivalent to \(\mu\le q\).

The proof establishes exactly these three assertions:

1. **Berry–Esseen effective-dimension lemma.** Define
   \[
   A_2=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i^2,\qquad A_3=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i^3,\qquad
   \rho=\frac{A_3}{A_2^{3/2}}.
   \]
   If \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and
   \[
   \rho\le \frac{5\sqrt2}{28},
   \]
   then
   \[
   \Pr[S\ge p]\ge p,
   \]
   equivalently \(\mu\le q\).

2. **Exact pair-merging formula.** Suppose two distinguished weights are \(a\ge b>0\), with \(a+b<p\), and let \(R\) be the residual sum from all other weights, independent of the Bernoulli variables used for these two weights. Define
   \[
   \mu_{\rm split}=\Pr[R+a\xi+b\eta<p],
   \]
   where \(\xi,\eta\) are independent Bernoulli\((p)\), and
   \[
   \mu_{\rm merge}=\Pr[R+(a+b)\zeta<p],
   \]
   where \(\zeta\) is Bernoulli\((p)\), independent of \(R\). Then
   \[
   \mu_{\rm split}-\mu_{\rm merge}
   =
   pq\Big(
   \Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
   -
   \Pr[R\in[p-b,p))
   \Big).
   \]

3. **Conditional reduction by good merges.** Fix \(p\in(0,1)\) and an integer \(N\ge1\). A **strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample of support \(n\)** means a list of \(n\) weights \(x_1,\dots,x_n\) such that
   \[
   0<x_i<p,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^n x_i=1,
   \]
   and, for independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(u_i\),
   \[
   \Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^n x_i u_i<p\Big]>q.
   \]
   Assume:
   - there are no strict reduced \(p\)-counterexamples of support at most \(N\);
   - every strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample of support greater than \(N\) contains some pair of weights \(a\ge b\) with \(a+b<p\) such that, for the residual sum \(R\) over the other weights,
     \[
     \Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
     \le
     \Pr[R\in[p-b,p)).
     \]
   Then no strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample exists at all.

The third assertion is conditional: it does **not** claim that the displayed good-merge inequality is always true.

## Proof

### 1. Berry–Esseen effective-dimension lemma

Let
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i,\qquad \mathbb E S=p\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=p.
\]
Set
\[
X_i=w_i(v_i-p).
\]
Then the \(X_i\) are independent, centered random variables, and
\[
S-p=\sum_{i=1}^m X_i.
\]

We invoke the standard Berry–Esseen theorem in the following form: if \(X_1,\dots,X_m\) are independent centered random variables, if
\[
\sigma^2=\sum_{i=1}^m \operatorname{Var}(X_i)>0,
\]
and if
\[
B=\sum_{i=1}^m \mathbb E|X_i|^3,
\]
then, with \(\Phi\) denoting the standard normal distribution function,
\[
\sup_x\left|\Pr\left[\frac{\sum_i X_i}{\sigma}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)\right|
\le
0.56\,\frac{B}{\sigma^3}.
\]

Here
\[
\operatorname{Var}(X_i)=w_i^2 pq,
\]
so
\[
\sigma^2=pq\sum_i w_i^2=pqA_2.
\]
Also,
\[
\mathbb E|X_i|^3
=
w_i^3\big(pq^3+qp^3\big)
=
w_i^3 pq(p^2+q^2),
\]
and hence
\[
B=pq(p^2+q^2)A_3.
\]
Therefore
\[
\frac{B}{\sigma^3}
=
\frac{pq(p^2+q^2)A_3}{(pqA_2)^{3/2}}
=
\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\rho.
\]

Applying Berry–Esseen at \(x=0\), and using \(\Phi(0)=1/2\), gives
\[
\Pr[S\le p]
=
\Pr\left[\frac{S-p}{\sqrt{pqA_2}}\le0\right]
\le
\frac12
+
0.56\,\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\rho.
\]
Since \(\mu=\Pr[S<p]\le \Pr[S\le p]\), it is enough to show
\[
0.56\,\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\rho
\le
\frac12-p.
\]

Assume now that \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and \(\rho\le 5\sqrt2/28\). Since \(0.56=14/25\),
\[
0.56\cdot \frac{5\sqrt2}{28}
=
\frac{\sqrt2}{10}.
\]
Thus it is enough to prove
\[
\frac{\sqrt2}{10}\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}
\le
\frac12-p.
\]
Let
\[
t=pq.
\]
For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), one has \(t\in[3/16,2/9]\). Also
\[
p^2+q^2=1-2t,
\qquad
(1-2p)^2=1-4t.
\]
The desired inequality is equivalent, after multiplying by positive quantities and squaring, to
\[
25t(1-4t)\ge 2(1-2t)^2.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
108t^2-33t+2\le0.
\]
But
\[
108t^2-33t+2
=
108\left(t-\frac1{12}\right)\left(t-\frac29\right).
\]
Since
\[
t\in\left[\frac3{16},\frac29\right]\subseteq \left[\frac1{12},\frac29\right],
\]
this quantity is indeed nonpositive. Therefore
\[
0.56\,\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\rho
\le
\frac12-p.
\]
Consequently
\[
\mu=\Pr[S<p]\le 1-p=q,
\]
and hence
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=1-\mu\ge p.
\]
This proves the Berry–Esseen effective-dimension lemma.

### 2. Exact pair-merging formula

Let \(a\ge b>0\) be two distinguished weights with \(a+b<p\). Let \(R\) be the residual sum over all other coordinates. Let \(\xi,\eta,\zeta\) be Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, independent of each other and of \(R\). Define
\[
\mu_{\rm split}=\Pr[R+a\xi+b\eta<p],
\]
and
\[
\mu_{\rm merge}=\Pr[R+(a+b)\zeta<p].
\]

Condition on \(R=r\), and put
\[
t=p-r.
\]
We compare
\[
\Pr[a\xi+b\eta<t]
\]
with
\[
\Pr[(a+b)\zeta<t].
\]

The split variable \(a\xi+b\eta\) takes the values
\[
0,\ b,\ a,\ a+b
\]
with probabilities
\[
q^2,\ qp,\ pq,\ p^2,
\]
respectively. The merged variable \((a+b)\zeta\) takes the values
\[
0,\ a+b
\]
with probabilities
\[
q,\ p.
\]

Therefore the conditional difference
\[
\Delta(t)=\Pr[a\xi+b\eta<t]-\Pr[(a+b)\zeta<t]
\]
is as follows:

- If \(t\le0\), both probabilities are \(0\), so \(\Delta(t)=0\).
- If \(0<t\le b\), the split variable is below \(t\) only when both Bernoulli variables are \(0\), so the split probability is \(q^2\), while the merged probability is \(q\). Hence
  \[
  \Delta(t)=q^2-q=-pq.
  \]
- If \(b<t\le a\), the split probability is \(q^2+qp=q\), and the merged probability is also \(q\), so \(\Delta(t)=0\).
- If \(a<t\le a+b\), the split probability is \(q^2+qp+pq=1-p^2\), while the merged probability is \(q\). Hence
  \[
  \Delta(t)=1-p^2-q=pq.
  \]
- If \(t>a+b\), both probabilities are \(1\), so \(\Delta(t)=0\).

Thus
\[
\Delta(t)=pq\,\mathbf 1_{\{a<t\le a+b\}}
-
pq\,\mathbf 1_{\{0<t\le b\}}.
\]
Substituting \(t=p-R\), we get
\[
\{a<t\le a+b\}
=
\{p-a-b\le R<p-a\},
\]
and
\[
\{0<t\le b\}
=
\{p-b\le R<p\}.
\]
Averaging over \(R\) gives
\[
\mu_{\rm split}-\mu_{\rm merge}
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
-
\Pr[R\in[p-b,p))
\Big).
\]
This proves the exact pair-merging formula.

### 3. Conditional reduction by good merges

Fix \(p\in(0,1)\), \(q=1-p\), and an integer \(N\ge1\).

Recall that a strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample of support \(n\) is a list of \(n\) weights
\[
x_1,\dots,x_n\in(0,p)
\]
with
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n x_i=1
\]
such that, for independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(u_i\),
\[
\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^n x_i u_i<p\Big]>q.
\]

Assume first that no strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample of support at most \(N\) exists. Assume also that every strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample of support greater than \(N\) contains a pair of weights \(a\ge b\) with \(a+b<p\) satisfying the good-merge inequality
\[
\Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
\le
\Pr[R\in[p-b,p)),
\]
where \(R\) is the residual sum over all coordinates except those two.

Suppose, for contradiction, that at least one strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample exists. Choose one with minimal support \(n\). By the first assumption, \(n>N\).

By the second assumption, this counterexample contains a pair \(a\ge b\) with \(a+b<p\) satisfying the good-merge inequality. Let \(\mu_{\rm split}\) be the bad probability of the original counterexample, and let \(\mu_{\rm merge}\) be the bad probability after replacing \(a,b\) by one weight \(a+b\).

By the pair-merging formula,
\[
\mu_{\rm split}-\mu_{\rm merge}
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
-
\Pr[R\in[p-b,p))
\Big).
\]
The good-merge inequality makes the right-hand side nonpositive, so
\[
\mu_{\rm split}-\mu_{\rm merge}\le0.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mu_{\rm merge}\ge \mu_{\rm split}.
\]
Since the original object was a strict counterexample,
\[
\mu_{\rm split}>q,
\]
and hence
\[
\mu_{\rm merge}>q.
\]

The merged list still has positive weights, still sums to \(1\), and remains reduced because all unchanged weights are \(<p\) and the new weight satisfies \(a+b<p\). Its support is \(n-1\). Thus the merged list is a strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample of smaller support than the chosen minimal one, a contradiction.

Therefore no strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample exists at all. This proves the conditional reduction. The argument is conditional on the good-merge hypothesis and does not assert that the good-merge inequality holds universally.

=1, if no strict reduced p-counterexample of support <=N exists and every strict reduced p-counterexample of support >N has a pair satisfying the good-merge inequality Pr[R in [p-a-b,p-a)) <= Pr[R in [p-b,p)), then no strict reduced p-counterexample exists. A strict reduced p-counterexample means a finite positive-weight list with all weights q. This is partial and conditional, not a characterization of all p.

=1, if no strict reduced p-counterexample of support <=N exists and every strict reduced p-counterexample of support >N has a pair satisfying the good-merge inequality Pr[R in [p-a-b,p-a)) <= Pr[R in [p-b,p)), then no strict reduced p-counterexample exists. description: writeup (partial): Strict reduced support-9 theorem in the upper interval: for p in [1/4,1/3), positive weights 0p]>p.
full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

We prove the following self-contained special case.

Let \(p\) be a real number with
\[
\frac14\le p<\frac13.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_9\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and let \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) be positive real numbers such that
\[
0<w_i<p\quad\text{for every }i,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^9 w_i=1.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

This is **not** the full original problem. It is the strict reduced support-\(9\) case on the interval \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\), with the stronger upper-tail event \(>p\) rather than \(\ge p\).

## Proof

Let
\[
q=1-p,\qquad r=\frac pq.
\]
Then \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\) is equivalent to
\[
\frac13\le r<\frac12.
\]

For \(A\subseteq[9]=\{1,\dots,9\}\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]
Define the losing family
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[9]: w(A)\le p\},
\]
and its layer counts
\[
D_j=D\cap\binom{[9]}j,\qquad d_j=|D_j|.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=\sum_{A\in D}p^{|A|}q^{9-|A|}
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j.
\]
Since \(1+r=1/q\), we have
\[
q=q^9(1+r)^8.
\]
Thus it is enough to prove
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j < (1+r)^8.
\]

Define the winning-pair graph \(W\) on vertex set \([9]\) by
\[
ij\in E(W)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad w_i+w_j>p.
\]
Let
\[
m=|E(W)|.
\]
Because every \(w_i<p\), all singletons are losing, and
\[
d_0=1,\qquad d_1=9,\qquad d_2=36-m.
\]

Also, no losing set has size at least \(7\). Indeed, if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge7\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so
\[
w(A^c)<2p<q,
\]
whereas \(w(A)\le p\) implies \(w(A^c)\ge q\), contradiction. Hence \(d_j=0\) for \(j\ge7\).

### 1. Layer bounds

A losing set cannot contain a winning pair. Hence every member of every \(D_j\) is an independent set in \(W\).

#### The \(D_3\) bound

The family \(D_3\) contains no three pairwise disjoint triples: three disjoint losing triples would partition \([9]\), giving total weight at most \(3p<1\), contradiction.

Count unordered partitions of \([9]\) into three triples. There are
\[
\frac{9!}{(3!)^3 3!}=280
\]
such partitions, and each fixed triple belongs to
\[
\frac{6!}{(3!)^2 2!}=10
\]
partitions. Since each partition contains at most two members of \(D_3\),
\[
10d_3\le 2\cdot280,
\]
so
\[
d_3\le56.
\]

#### The \(D_4\) bounds

First, \(D_4\) is intersecting. If \(A,B\in D_4\) were disjoint, with leftover vertex \(z\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z\le 2p+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible.

Thus \(D_4\) is an intersecting family of \(W\)-independent \(4\)-sets.

The graph \(W\) is a threshold graph: it is represented by vertex weights \(w_i\) and threshold \(p\), with edge condition \(w_i+w_j>p\). Equivalently, every induced subgraph has either an isolated minimum-weight vertex or a dominating maximum-weight vertex, yielding a creation sequence by isolated/dominating vertex additions.

The following finite threshold-graph enumeration gives, for \(m=|E(W)|\),
\[
d_4\le I_m,
\]
where
\[
I_0=56,\ I_1=50,\ I_2=45,\ I_3=41,\ I_4=38,\ I_5=36,\qquad I_m=35\quad(m\ge6).
\]

We also need an independent weighted-degree bound. Let
\[
\mathcal C=\{A^c:A\in D_4\}\subseteq\binom{[9]}5.
\]
Because \(D_4\) is intersecting, \(\mathcal C\) is \(2\)-intersecting. Also, for every \(B\in\mathcal C\),
\[
w(B)=1-w(A)\ge q.
\]
Let \(\Delta_i\) be the degree of vertex \(i\) in \(\mathcal C\), ordered so that
\[
\Delta_1\ge\Delta_2\ge\cdots\ge\Delta_9.
\]
Then
\[
q|\mathcal C|
\le \sum_{B\in\mathcal C}w(B)
=\sum_i w_i\Delta_i.
\]
Given \(0<w_i<p\) and \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the dot product with nonincreasing coefficients \(\Delta_i\) is maximized by placing mass \(p,p,p,1-3p\) on the four largest coefficients. Hence
\[
\sum_i w_i\Delta_i
\le p(\Delta_1+\Delta_2+\Delta_3)+(1-3p)\Delta_4.
\]
For each vertex \(i\), the link
\[
\mathcal C_i=\{B\setminus\{i\}:B\in\mathcal C,\ i\in B\}
\]
is an intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of an \(8\)-set, so \(|\mathcal C_i|\le35\), because the \(70\) four-subsets of an \(8\)-set split into \(35\) complementary pairs. Thus \(\Delta_i\le35\) for all \(i\), and therefore
\[
q d_4=q|\mathcal C|\le35.
\]
Since \(1/q=1+r\),
\[
d_4\le \lfloor 35(1+r)\rfloor.
\]

Combining the two \(D_4\) bounds,
\[
d_4\le C_m(r):=\min\{I_m,\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor\}.
\]

#### The \(D_5\) bound

Let \(A\in D_5\), and put \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=4\). If \(B\) had a perfect matching consisting of two losing pairs \(e_1,e_2\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le3p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence \(W[B]\) meets all three perfect matchings of \(K_4\), so \(W[B]\) contains at least three winning edges.

Therefore \(d_5=0\) if \(m\le2\). If \(m=3\), every such \(B\) must contain all three winning edges; at most \(6\) four-sets can do this, so \(d_5\le6\).

For \(m\ge4\), use complements. The complements of members of \(D_5\) form a \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-sets: if \(B_1,B_2\) were two such complements with \(|B_1\cap B_2|\le1\), then
\[
w(B_1\cup B_2)=w(B_1)+w(B_2)-w(B_1\cap B_2)>2q-p=2-3p>1,
\]
impossible. The finite certificate below verifies that any \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\) has size at most \(21\). Thus
\[
d_5\le B_m:=
\begin{cases}
0,&m\le2,\\
6,&m=3,\\
21,&m\ge4.
\end{cases}
\]

#### The \(D_6\) bound

Let \(A\in D_6\), and put \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=3\). If some pair \(e\subset B\) were losing, and \(z=B\setminus e\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e)+w_z\le2p+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence every pair inside \(B\) is winning, so \(B\) is a triangle of \(W\).

Therefore
\[
d_6\le \tau(W),
\]
where \(\tau(W)\) is the number of triangles in \(W\). If a graph has \(m\) edges, then its number of triangles is at most
\[
T_m=\binom a3+\binom b2,
\]
where
\[
m=\binom a2+b,\qquad 0\le b<a.
\]
This follows by induction on \(m\): choose a vertex of degree \(d\le a-1\), remove it, and use the elementary inequality
\[
T_m-T_{m-d}\ge \binom d2.
\]

Also, complements of members of \(D_6\) form a \(2\)-intersecting family of triples, hence have size at most \(7\). Therefore
\[
d_6\le G_m:=\min\{7,T_m\}.
\]

### 2. Deficiency cascade

For \(A\in D_k\), define its normalized deficiency
\[
\varepsilon(A)=\frac{p-w(A)}p\ge0,
\]
and let
\[
E_k=\sum_{A\in D_k}\varepsilon(A).
\]

For \(k\ge2\), if \(B\in D_{k+1}\), then
\[
\sum_{\substack{A\subset B\\ |A|=k}}\bigl(p-w(A)\bigr)
=(k+1)p-k w(B)
=p+k(p-w(B)).
\]
After dividing by \(p\) and summing over \(B\in D_{k+1}\), each \(A\in D_k\) is counted at most \(9-k\) times, so
\[
d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le (9-k)E_k.
\]

For pairs,
\[
pE_2=\sum_{ij\notin E(W)}(p-w_i-w_j).
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{i<j}(p-w_i-w_j)=36p-8
\]
and each winning pair contributes more than \(-p\), we get
\[
E_2\le 28+m-\frac8r.
\]

Applying the cascade for \(k=2,3,4,5\) gives
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le 7E_2
\le 196+7m-\frac{56}{r}.
\]

### 3. The final LP inequality

For fixed \(m\) and \(r\), every actual layer vector satisfies
\[
0\le d_3\le56,\quad
0\le d_4\le C_m(r),\quad
0\le d_5\le B_m,\quad
0\le d_6\le G_m,
\]
and
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le 196+7m-\frac{56}{r}.
\]

Define \(U_m(r)\) to be the maximum of
\[
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+x_3r^3+x_4r^4+x_5r^5+x_6r^6
\]
over all real \(x_3,x_4,x_5,x_6\) satisfying the same bounds and cascade inequality.

The exact finite certificate below proves
\[
U_m(r)<(1+r)^8
\]
for every integer \(0\le m\le36\) and every real \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\). The endpoint \(r=1/2\) corresponds to \(p=1/3\), which is outside the claimed range, but checking it is harmless.

The certificate has three parts:

1. It verifies the finite \(2\)-intersection bounds used for \(d_5,d_6\).
2. It enumerates all threshold graphs on \(9\) vertices by creation sequence and verifies the table \(I_m\).
3. It enumerates all LP vertices on each interval where \(\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor\) is constant, and proves the resulting rational gap functions positive by Sturm root counting.

```python
import sympy as sp
from itertools import combinations, product
from math import comb as _comb

r = sp.symbols("r")

def C(n, k):
    if k < 0 or k > n:
        return 0
    return _comb(n, k)

# ---------- exact maximum independent set / clique routines ----------

def max_clique_size(adj):
    """
    Exact branch-and-bound maximum clique.
    adj[i] is a bitset of neighbors of i.
    """
    N = len(adj)
    full = (1 << N) - 1
    best = 0

    def color_sort(P):
        # Greedy coloring of the candidate graph gives a valid clique upper bound.
        vertices = []
        colors = []
        U = P
        color = 0
        while U:
            color += 1
            Q = U
            while Q:
                lb = Q & -Q
                v = lb.bit_length() - 1
                vertices.append(v)
                colors.append(color)
                U &= ~lb
                Q &= ~lb
                Q &= ~adj[v]
                Q &= U
        return vertices, colors

    def expand(size, P):
        nonlocal best
        if P == 0:
            best = max(best, size)
            return
        vertices, colors = color_sort(P)
        for idx in range(len(vertices) - 1, -1, -1):
            if size + colors[idx] <= best:
                return
            v = vertices[idx]
            expand(size + 1, P & adj[v])
            P &= ~(1 << v)

    expand(0, full)
    return best

def max_independent_size(conflict):
    N = len(conflict)
    full = (1 << N) - 1
    comp = [(full & ~(1 << i)) & ~conflict[i] for i in range(N)]
    return max_clique_size(comp)

def masks_of_size(n, k):
    out = []
    for S in combinations(range(n), k):
        mask = 0
        for i in S:
            mask |= 1 << i
        out.append(mask)
    return out

def max_t_intersecting(n, k, t):
    sets = masks_of_size(n, k)
    N = len(sets)
    conflict = [0] * N
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if (sets[i] & sets[j]).bit_count() < t:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i
    return max_independent_size(conflict)

assert max_t_intersecting(9, 3, 2) == 7
assert max_t_intersecting(9, 4, 2) == 21

# ---------- threshold-graph enumeration for the D4 bound ----------

def threshold_graphs(n=9):
    # Creation sequence: each new vertex is isolated or dominating
    # relative to earlier vertices.
    seen = set()
    for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=n - 1):
        adj = [0] * n
        for v, bit in enumerate(bits, start=1):
            if bit:
                for u in range(v):
                    adj[v] |= 1 << u
                    adj[u] |= 1 << v
        tup = tuple(adj)
        if tup not in seen:
            seen.add(tup)
            yield tup

def edge_count(adj):
    return sum(a.bit_count() for a in adj) // 2

def max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj):
    sets = []
    for S in combinations(range(9), 4):
        independent = True
        for i, j in combinations(S, 2):
            if (adj[i] >> j) & 1:
                independent = False
                break
        if independent:
            mask = 0
            for i in S:
                mask |= 1 << i
            sets.append(mask)

    N = len(sets)
    conflict = [0] * N
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if sets[i] & sets[j] == 0:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i

    return max_independent_size(conflict)

I = [56, 50, 45, 41, 38, 36] + [35] * 31

max_by_m = [0] * 37
hit = [False] * 37

for adj in threshold_graphs(9):
    m = edge_count(adj)
    hit[m] = True
    val = max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj)
    max_by_m[m] = max(max_by_m[m], val)

assert all(hit)
assert all(max_by_m[m] <= I[m] for m in range(37))

# ---------- layer caps ----------

def tri_max(m):
    a = 0
    while C(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - C(a, 2)
    return C(a, 3) + C(b, 2)

def B_cap(m):
    if m <= 2:
        return 0
    if m == 3:
        return 6
    return 21

def G_cap(m):
    return min(7, tri_max(m))

# ---------- exact Sturm positivity checks ----------

def assert_positive_on(expr, lo, hi, tag):
    # The LP vertices can involve 1/r; multiply by r>0.
    expr = sp.together(expr * r)
    num, den = sp.together(expr).as_numer_denom()
    num = sp.expand(num)
    den = sp.expand(den)

    den_poly = sp.Poly(den, r)
    assert den_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and den_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert den_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

    num_poly = sp.Poly(num, r)
    assert num_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and num_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert num_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

def intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r >= 0, with r>0.
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return None
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    lo2 = max(lo, root)
    if lo2 > hi:
        return None
    return lo2, hi

def intersect_le_L(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r <= 0, with r>0.
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return lo, hi
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    hi2 = min(hi, root)
    if lo > hi2:
        return None
    return lo, hi2

breaks = [
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(12, 35),
    sp.Rational(13, 35),
    sp.Rational(14, 35),
    sp.Rational(15, 35),
    sp.Rational(16, 35),
    sp.Rational(17, 35),
    sp.Rational(1, 2),
]

target = (1 + r) ** 8

cost = [
    sp.Rational(1, 1),
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
]
value = [r**3, r**4, r**5, r**6]

for m in range(37):
    for lo, hi in zip(breaks[:-1], breaks[1:]):
        # On this interval, use the right-end value as a safe relaxation
        # of floor(35(1+r)).
        K = int(sp.floor(35 * (1 + hi)))
        C4 = min(I[m], K)

        caps = [56, C4, B_cap(m), G_cap(m)]
        base = 1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2
        Lambda0 = sp.Rational(196 + 7*m)

        # Vertices with all variables fixed at bounds.
        for mask in range(16):
            xs = []
            fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)
            for i in range(4):
                xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if ((mask >> i) & 1) else sp.Rational(0)
                xs.append(xi)
                fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

            iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
            if iv is not None:
                a, b = iv
                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("fixed", m, lo, hi, mask))

        # Vertices with the budget active and one variable free.
        for free in range(4):
            others = [i for i in range(4) if i != free]
            for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=3):
                xs = [None] * 4
                fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)

                for bit, i in zip(bits, others):
                    xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if bit else sp.Rational(0)
                    xs[i] = xi
                    fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

                iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                iv = intersect_le_L(
                    a,
                    b,
                    Lambda0 - fixed_cost - cost[free] * sp.Rational(caps[free])
                )
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                Lambda_expr = Lambda0 - sp.Rational(56, 1) / r
                xfree = (Lambda_expr - fixed_cost) / cost[free]
                xs[free] = xfree

                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("free", m, lo, hi, free, bits))

print("finite combinatorial assertions and exact LP/Sturm certificate passed")
```

The LP vertex enumeration is exhaustive because the feasible region has four variables, box constraints, and one additional linear budget constraint. At a vertex, either the budget is inactive and all four variables are at bounds, or the budget is active and at least three variables are at bounds. The code checks exactly those cases.

Therefore, for the actual layer vector,
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j
\le U_m(r)
<(1+r)^8.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_jr^j
<q^9(1+r)^8
=q.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]
=1-\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
>1-q
=p.
\]
This proves the claim.

  "original_problem": "Let $w_1, \\ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let \n$v_1, \\ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that\n\\[\\Pr\\Big[\\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\\geq p\\Big]\\geq p?\\]",

This is only the strict reduced support-9 case on p in [1/4,1/3), not the full original problem.",
